> - $10^{-10} \le E < 10^{-4}$ — borderline; needs domain analysis.
> - $E \ge 1$ — not significant; expected by chance.

### 2.4 Real-world Utility in Engineering & Biology

- **Drug discovery pipelines** screen millions of compound-target pairs daily; BLAST homology is used to **transfer functional annotation** from model organisms to human drug targets.
- **Metagenomics** of soil/oceans uses BLAST to classify **billions of unknown reads** against curated databases like **NCBI-nr**.
- **Phylogenetic inference** of novel pathogens (e.g., during the COVID-19 outbreak) relied on BLAST to identify SARS-CoV-2 within hours of sequencing.
- **Primer design in PCR** and **probe design in microarrays** use heuristic local alignment to avoid off-target hybridisation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Raw Score *S* to E-value

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation is the standard KTU board-examination derivation. Practice writing it out completely; the **boundary conditions** and the **transformation of variables** are the marks-bearing parts.

**Step 1 — Starting point: the Karlin–Altschul statistical framework.**

For a local alignment with no gaps and substitution scores drawn from a random-independent model, the **distribution of the maximum score *S*** over a search space of effective size $\mu = m \cdot n$ follows a **Gumbel extreme-value distribution**.

$$P(S \le x) = \exp\!\left(-K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda x}\right)$$

Here *K* and *λ* are the Karlin–Altschul statistical parameters. Their values are **scoring-matrix-dependent**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &> 0 \quad \text{(scale parameter)} \\
K &> 0 \quad \text{(prefactor controlling the density of high scores)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Derive the P-value (the probability of a single score ≥ *S*).**

Using the survival function of the Gumbel distribution:

$$P(S \ge x) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}\right)$$

**Step 3 — For very small probabilities** ($E \ll 1$), the approximation $1 - e^{-z} \approx z$ applies:

$$P(S \ge S_{\text{obs}}) \approx K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S_{\text{obs}}}$$

**Step 4 — Define the E-value** as the expected number of alignments with score ≥ *S*. For one search the E-value equals the P-value:

$$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$$

This is the **primary KTU formula**. Substitute *m* = 250 (query length), *n* = 10⁹ (database size), *S* = 60 bits, and typical *K* ≈ 0.041, *λ* ≈ 0.267 for BLOSUM62 to obtain:

$$E = 0.041 \times 250 \times 10^{9} \times e^{-0.267 \times 60}$$

**Step 5 — Numerically evaluate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda S & = 0.267 \times 60 = 16.02 \\
e^{-16.02} & \approx 1.12 \times 10^{-7} \\
E & \approx 0.041 \times 2.5 \times 10^{11} \times 1.12 \times 10^{-7} \\
E & \approx 1.15 \times 10^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

An E-value of **~1150** indicates the alignment is statistically **not significant** for a search of this size.

**Step 6 — Derive the Bit Score transformation.**

We want a score **independent of database size**. Solve the E-value equation for *S*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E & = K m n e^{-\lambda S} \\
\dfrac{E}{K m n} & = e^{-\lambda S} \\
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K m n}{E}\right) & = \lambda S \\
S & = \dfrac{\ln(K m n / E)}{\lambda} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For *E* = 1 (a single expected chance hit), define the bit score *S'*:

$$S' = \dfrac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$

The two **logarithm transformations** are what the KTU paper checker verifies — students frequently forget the $\ln K$ term and lose 2 marks.

**Step 7 — Convert between E-value and Bit Score.**

Starting from $E = m \cdot n \cdot 2^{-S'}$, take natural logarithms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln E & = \ln(mn) - S' \ln 2 \\
S' & = \dfrac{\ln(mn) - \ln E}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{\ln(mn/E)}{\ln 2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **operational formula** used by NCBI to print Bit Scores in BLAST output.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **Problem (KTU Dec 2022 style).** A BLASTP search of a 350-residue protein against the Swiss-Prot database (5 × 10⁷ residues) returns an alignment with raw score *S* = 78. For BLOSUM62, *K* = 0.041 and *λ* = 0.267. Compute the E-value and comment on its significance.

**Solution (full marking scheme).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m & = 350 \\
n & = 5 \times 10^{7} \\
m \cdot n & = 350 \times 5 \times 10^{7} = 1.75 \times 10^{10} \\
\lambda S & = 0.267 \times 78 = 20.826 \\
e^{-\lambda S} & = e^{-20.826} \approx 8.95 \times 10^{-10} \\
E & = 0.041 \times 1.75 \times 10^{10} \times 8.95 \times 10^{-10} \\
E & = 0.041 \times 15.66 \\
E & \approx 0.642 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation.** $E \approx 0.64$ means we expect **0.64 false-positive hits** of this score in a random database. This is **borderline insignificant** (the KTU benchmark threshold is $E < 0.05$ for "significant"). Therefore the homology is **suspect** and would require further experimental validation.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — FASTA vs BLAST

| Parameter | FASTA | BLAST |
|---|---|---|
| Year & Authors | Pearson & Lipman, 1988 | Altschul et al., 1990 |
| Word length (*k* / *w*) | 1–2 (protein), 4–6 (DNA) | 3 (protein), 11 (DNA in BLASTN) |
| Substitution matrix | PAM250, BLOSUM50 | BLOSUM62 (default) |
| Stages | 4 (ktup, hot-spot, rescan, banded DP) | 5 (word list, hits, ungapped ext, gapped ext, statistics) |
| Initial scoring | Density of ktup matches | Sum of substitution scores ≥ *T* |
| Gaps in output | Yes (Smith–Waterman band) | Yes (Gapped BLAST / BLAST2) |
| Speed | Fast | **Faster** (sub-linear in *n*) |
| Sensitivity | High for strong homology | **Higher** for weak/distal homology |
| Selectivity | Slightly more false positives | Slightly more conservative |
| E-value reporting | No (uses Poisson *z*-score) | **Yes** (Karlin–Altschul *E*) |
| Best use case | Long strong alignments | **Most modern homology searches** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — FASTA Algorithm (4-Stage Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Query Sequence] --> B[Decompose into ktup words]
    B --> C[Build Hash Lookup Table]
    C --> D[Scan Database for ktup matches]
    D --> E{Density of matches in window?}
    E -- High density --> F[Mark as best init1 region]
    E -- Low density --> G[Discard region]
    F --> H[Re-score top N regions with BLOSUM/PAM]
    H --> I[Join overlapping high-score regions]
    I --> J[initn score computed]
    J --> K[Apply banded Smith-Waterman DP]
    K --> L[Final opt score and alignment]
    L --> M[Output ranked list]
    G --> N[Move to next database sequence]
    N --> D
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — BLAST Algorithm (5-Stage Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Query Sequence] --> R[Generate overlapping words of length w]
    R --> S[Score each word against substitution matrix]
    S --> T[Keep words with score above threshold T]
    T --> U[Generate neighborhood words above T]
    U --> V[Scan database for word hits]
    V --> W[Record hit position i query and j database]
    W --> X[Ungapped extension in both directions]
    X --> Y{Cumulative score still high?}
    Y -- Yes --> X
    Y -- No --> Z[Save maximal segment as HSP]
    Z --> AA[HSP score above Sg threshold?]
    AA -- Yes --> AB[Perform gapped extension with banded DP]
    AA -- No --> AC[Discard HSP]
    AB --> AD[Compute bit score and E-value]
    AD --> AE[Sort and output alignments]
    AC --> V
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Relationship Between Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input_Layer
        A1[FASTA File Query]
        A2[NCBI Database e.g. nr swissprot]
    end
    subgraph Heuristic_Engine
        B1[Word Generation]
        B2[Hash Table Lookup]
        B3[High-Scoring Segment Detection]
        B4[Statistical Significance Filter]
    end
    subgraph Output_Layer
        C1[Ranked Alignments]
        C2[E-values and Bit Scores]
        C3[Annotated Hits]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Information Flow of an E-value Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RawScore[Raw Score S] --> LambdaS[Multiply by lambda]
    LambdaS --> Exp[Apply exponential decay e power minus lambda S]
    Exp --> Multiply[Multiply by K times m times n]
    Multiply --> Evalue[Final E-value]
    RawScore --> Bit[Transform to Bit Score using ln K and ln 2]
    Bit --> BitOut[Print Bit Score in output]
    Evalue --> Decision{E value below threshold}
    Decision -- Yes --> Sig[Report as significant hit]
    Decision -- No --> NonSig[Discard or flag as suspect]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a heuristic algorithm in bioinformatics. Why is dynamic programming (Smith–Waterman) not used for routine database searches?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A heuristic algorithm is a problem-solving shortcut that finds an approximate but biologically meaningful alignment in significantly less time than exhaustive methods, by exploiting empirical rules such as shared short words.
- **(1 mark)** Smith–Waterman requires $O(m \times n)$ time, which becomes infeasible for large databases (e.g., GenBank's 3.5 × 10¹² bp).
- **(1 mark)** Heuristics like BLAST reduce this to near-linear time by **pre-filtering** sequences using short identical words, sacrificing the guarantee of finding the optimal alignment for **computational tractability**.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is an E-value in BLAST output? State its formula and interpret $E = 10^{-15}$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The E-value (Expect value) is the number of alignments with a score ≥ the observed score that would be expected to occur **by chance** in a database of the given size.
- **(1 mark)** $E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$, where *K* and *λ* are Karlin–Altschul parameters, *m* is query length, *n* is database size, and *S* is the raw score.
- **(1 mark)** $E = 10^{-15}$ means we expect $10^{-15}$ chance hits of this score; this is **astronomically significant** and the alignment is virtually certain to be a true biological homology.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write**
> - **Skipping the derivation of the E-value from first principles** costs 3–4 marks in long-answer questions. Always start from the Gumbel distribution and show the algebra.
> - **Confusing E-value with P-value.** E = expected *number* of chance hits; P = probability of *at least one* chance hit. They are related by $P = 1 - e^{-E}$, *not* equal.
> - **Forgetting the $\ln K$ term** in the Bit Score equation loses 2 marks. The full formula is $S' = (\lambda S - \ln K) / \ln 2$, not $\lambda S / \ln 2$.
> - **Mislabelling the parameters.** *m* = query length, *n* = database length — do *not* swap them.
> - **No units in the final answer.** Always state "the E-value is dimensionless" or "the Bit Score is in bits".

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** Describe the **BLAST algorithm** in detail, listing its five main stages. Explain how it differs from FASTA in handling word matches. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **(1 mark)** Define BLAST: a heuristic local alignment search tool published by Altschul et al. in 1990; widely used at NCBI for sequence homology search.
- **Stage 1 (1 mark) — Word list compilation:** The query sequence of length *L* is decomposed into overlapping words of length *w* (default 3 for proteins, 11 for DNA). Each word is scored against every possible word of the same length using the substitution matrix (BLOSUM62). Words scoring above a threshold *T* (default 11 for proteins) are retained, along with their **neighborhood** of similar words.
- **Stage 2 (1 mark) — Database scanning:** The database is scanned linearly. Wherever a query word matches a database word (the **word hit**), a pointer is recorded at coordinates (*i*, *j*).
- **Stage 3 (1 mark) — Ungapped extension:** Each word hit is extended in both directions without gaps. The cumulative score is tracked. Extension stops when the score drops below the maximum found so far by a defined margin *Xg*. The resulting maximal segment is the **High-scoring Segment Pair (HSP)**.
- **Stage 4 (1 mark) — Gapped extension (BLAST2):** HSPs with score ≥ *Sg* are passed to a gapped alignment routine using dynamic programming restricted to a diagonal band (~50 cells wide), based on the Karlin–Altschul theory.
- **Stage 5 (1 mark) — Statistical evaluation:** Each alignment is assigned a bit score $S' = (\lambda S - \ln K)/\ln 2$ and an E-value $E = K m n 2^{-S'}$. Alignments are sorted by E-value and reported.
- **(1 mark) FASTA vs BLAST difference:** FASTA uses **fixed identical words of length *ktup*** (e.g., 1 or 2 for proteins) and looks for **clusters** of matches. BLAST uses **scored words above a threshold *T*** and **neighbourhood words**, so it tolerates conservative substitutions within the seed word — making it **more sensitive** for divergent sequences.

**(b)** Derive the **Karlin–Altschul E-value formula** from the Gumbel extreme-value distribution. A 400-residue query is searched against a database of 2 × 10⁸ residues. An alignment produces a raw score *S* = 65. Using $K = 0.04$ and $\lambda = 0.27$ (BLOSUM62), compute the E-value and comment on the significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** Begin with the cumulative distribution of the maximum alignment score *S* under a random model:

$$P(S \le x) = \exp\!\left(-K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda x}\right)$$

- **(1 mark)** Apply the survival function:

$$P(S \ge x) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-K m n e^{-\lambda S}\right)$$

- **(1 mark)** For small probabilities, use $1 - e^{-z} \approx z$:

$$P(S \ge S) \approx K m n e^{-\lambda S}$$

- **(1 mark)** Define the E-value as the expected number of chance hits (numerically equal to the P-value for one search):

$$\boxed{E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}}$$

**Numerical evaluation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m & = 400 \\
n & = 2 \times 10^{8} \\
m \cdot n & = 8 \times 10^{10} \\
\lambda S & = 0.27 \times 65 = 17.55 \\
e^{-17.55} & \approx 2.41 \times 10^{-8} \\
E & = 0.04 \times 8 \times 10^{10} \times 2.41 \times 10^{-8} \\
E & = 0.04 \times 1928 \\
E & \approx 77.1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (final 0 marks but essential):** $E \approx 77$ means we expect **77 false-positive alignments** of this score by chance alone. Since $E \gg 1$, the alignment is **not statistically significant**; the hit should be **discarded** unless supported by additional biological evidence.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **FASTA algorithm** step-by-step. What role does the substitution matrix (e.g., PAM250) play in the rescan step? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Stage 1 (1.5 marks) — ktup decomposition:** The query is split into overlapping words of length *ktup*. For proteins, *ktup* = 1 or 2; for DNA, *ktup* = 4–6. A **lookup table** is constructed mapping each word to its position(s) in the query.
- **Stage 2 (1.5 marks) — Hot-spot identification:** The database is scanned for exact matches of these words. A **dot plot** is generated. The 10 regions (or *N* regions, configurable) with the **highest density** of dots are flagged as the *init1* best initial regions.
- **Stage 3 (1.5 marks) — Rescan with substitution matrix:** Each candidate region is re-evaluated using a **scoring matrix** such as **PAM250** (for evolutionary distant sequences) or **BLOSUM50**. The matrix assigns scores to all possible amino-acid pairs, rewarding conservative substitutions (e.g., I↔L) and penalising radical changes. This step yields the *initn* score.
- **Stage 4 (1.5 marks) — Banded dynamic programming:** A narrow band of **Smith–Waterman** dynamic programming is applied only across the *initn* region, producing the *opt* (optimal) score and final alignment.
- **Role of PAM250 (1 mark):** PAM250 captures **evolutionary substitution probabilities over 250 PAM units of divergence**. It is more permissive than BLOSUM62 and helps detect **distant homologs** in the rescan step where exact word matches would have been missed.

**(b)** Discuss the **conceptual differences between the E-value and P-value** in BLAST. A BLAST search reports a bit score $S' = 120$ against a database of effective length 10¹⁰. Compute the corresponding E-value. Is the hit significant? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual discussion (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark) E-value:** Expected **number** of alignments with a score ≥ observed that would arise by chance in the database of size *n*:

$$E = m \cdot n \cdot 2^{-S'}$$

- **(1 mark) P-value:** Probability of observing **at least one** chance alignment with score ≥ *S*:

$$P = 1 - e^{-E}$$

- **(1 mark) Relationship and pitfalls:** For $E \ll 1$, $P \approx E$ (a useful first-order approximation). However, the **E-value depends on database size** — increasing *n* tenfold increases *E* tenfold — while the **P-value is size-independent** and therefore more transferable across different searches. Students often state $E = P$ — this is *only* a small-*E* approximation.

**Numerical evaluation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E & = m \cdot n \cdot 2^{-S'} \\
E & = 10^{10} \times 2^{-120} \\
2^{120} & \approx 1.33 \times 10^{36} \\
E & \approx \dfrac{10^{10}}{1.33 \times 10^{36}} \\
E & \approx 7.5 \times 10^{-27} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** $E \approx 7.5 \times 10^{-27}$ is **astronomically significant** — far below the conventional $10^{-4}$ cutoff and well into the $E < 10^{-50}$ regime of "virtually certain homology". The hit is **highly significant** and can be reported with confidence as a true biological homolog.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for ESE**

- **Heuristic algorithms** (FASTA, BLAST) trade optimality for speed; they exploit identical or near-identical short words (k-tuples) to filter the database.
- **FASTA = 4 stages:** (1) ktup lookup, (2) hot-spot density, (3) substitution-matrix rescan, (4) banded Smith–Waterman.
- **BLAST = 5 stages:** (1) word list with threshold *T*, (2) database scan for hits, (3) ungapped extension → HSP, (4) gapped extension (BLAST2), (5) E-value and bit score reporting.
- **Default word length:** *w* = 3 for proteins, *w* = 11 for DNA in BLASTN.
- **E-value formula (must memorise):** $E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$.
- **Bit score formula (must memorise):** $S' = (\lambda S - \ln K) / \ln 2$.
- **Conversion shortcut:** $E = m \cdot n \cdot 2^{-S'}$.
- **P-value relation:** $P = 1 - e^{-E}$; for $E \ll 1$, $P \approx E$.
- **Significance thresholds:** $E < 10^{-4}$ acceptable; $E < 10^{-10}$ strong; $E < 10^{-50}$ near-identical.
- **BLAST is faster and more sensitive** than FASTA for distant homologs because of neighbourhood-word expansion.
- **FASTA is preferred for long, strong alignments** where exact word matching is sufficient.
- **Karlin–Altschul parameters** *K* and *λ* are matrix-dependent; typical values for BLOSUM62 are $K \approx 0.041$, $\lambda \approx 0.267$.
- **Substitution matrices:** BLOSUM62 (BLAST default), PAM250 (FASTA default, more permissive for distant homologs).
- **HSP = High-scoring Segment Pair** — ungapped maximal segment in BLAST Stage 3.
- **ktup** is FASTA's term for word length; *w* is BLAST's — these are **different parameters** even when numerically equal.
- **NCBI BLAST variants:** BLASTN (DNA-DNA), BLASTP (protein-protein), BLASTX (DNA query vs protein DB), TBLASTN (protein query vs translated DNA DB), TBLASTX (translated DNA vs translated DNA).
- **Score ($S$) vs Bit Score ($S'$):** $S$ depends on matrix and gap penalties; $S'$ is normalised and database-size independent.
- **Multiple testing:** For a database with $n$ independent comparisons, the family-wise E-value is amplified; use Bonferroni-style threshold $E < 0.05 / n$ for genome-wide significance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT615 - BIOINFORMATICS TECHNIQUES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** FASTA and BLAST

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 FASTA (Fast All)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> FASTA is a **DNA and protein sequence alignment software package** developed by **David J. Lipman and William R. Pearson (1985, refined 1988)** that uses a **heuristic (approximate) word-based method** to perform rapid pairwise sequence comparison against a sequence database. It is the precursor heuristic method to BLAST and remains widely used for its sensitivity at finding global and local alignments.

- **"FASTA"** originally stood for **"FAST-All"** (FAST-ALL, because it could align any type of sequence — DNA, RNA, or protein).
- It performs both **local** and **global** alignments.
- The method uses a **word (k-tuple / ktup) lookup strategy** to identify regions of similarity faster than the full dynamic programming (Needleman–Wunsch / Smith–Waterman) approach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Library Search):**
> Imagine you walk into a giant library of 200 million books and want to find any book that contains a sentence similar to "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog". Instead of reading every single book word-by-word, you first scan only short chunks (say, 4 consecutive words). If a book contains your 4-word chunk anywhere, you flag it. Then you only deeply read the flagged books. **FASTA does exactly this** — it scans short "ktup" words, flags candidates, and then performs detailed alignment only on promising matches.

### 1.2 BLAST (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> BLAST (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool) is a **heuristic algorithm for local sequence alignment** developed by **Stephen F. Altschul, Warren Gish, Webb Miller, Eugene W. Myers, and David J. Lipman (1990, refined 1997)** at the NCBI. It identifies regions of local similarity between a query sequence and sequences in a database by first finding short "words" of high score, then extending them to high-scoring segment pairs (HSPs).

- **BLAST** finds **High-scoring Segment Pairs (HSPs)** — pairs of sequence fragments (one from the query, one from the database) whose alignment score exceeds a threshold **S**.
- It is **far faster** than Smith–Waterman (full DP) — often **>10,000× faster** — while sacrificing only a small amount of sensitivity.
- It is the **most widely used bioinformatics tool in the world**, hosted publicly at **NCBI BLAST** and embedded in **EBI, Ensembl, UniProt, and hundreds of genomics pipelines**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plagiarism Detector):**
> Think of BLAST as an automated plagiarism checker used by universities. The professor (query) submits a 5-page essay. The system first looks for **exact short phrases** (e.g., "machine learning algorithms"). Every student paper (database entry) containing that phrase is flagged. The system then **extends** the comparison to surrounding sentences to confirm and score the plagiarism. BLAST does the same: it finds short exact words, then extends left and right to build High-scoring Segment Pairs.

### 1.3 Why FASTA and BLAST Matter (KTU Engineering Context)

> [!NOTE]
> - Both are **heuristic (approximate)** — they trade perfect sensitivity for **massive speed**.
> - They are essential in **comparative genomics, gene annotation, drug target identification, evolutionary studies, and metagenomics**.
> - For a B.Tech biotechnologist, mastery of FASTA/BLAST is mandatory — these are the **first tools used** when a new gene/protein is sequenced.

### 1.4 Key Distinction at a Glance

| Feature | FASTA | BLAST |
|---|---|---|
| Year | 1985 (Lipman & Pearson) | 1990 (Altschul et al.) |
| Approach | **Word-based + banded DP** | **Word-based + seeded extension** |
| ktup (k-tuple) | **6** (DNA default), **2** (protein default) | **3** (protein default, also 1 for exact), **11** (DNA default) |
| Speed | Slower than BLAST | Faster |
| Sensitivity | Slightly higher in some cases | Excellent balance |
| Output unit | **init1, initn, opt score** | **Bit score, E-value** |
| Variants | FASTA, FASTX/Y, TFASTX/Y | BLASTN, BLASTP, BLASTX, TBLASTN, TBLASTX |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often confuse **ktup of FASTA** (smaller, e.g., 2 or 6) with **word size W of BLAST** (larger, e.g., 3 or 11). They are **conceptually opposite in size** by design — smaller ktup = higher sensitivity, larger word size = higher speed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 FASTA — Step-by-Step Algorithm

The FASTA algorithm operates in **four to five distinct phases**. Understanding each is essential for KTU questions.

#### **Phase 1: Identify Common k-tuples (Word Finding)**
- The query sequence is broken into overlapping short words of length **ktup** (k-tuple).
- For **DNA**: default ktup = **6** (sometimes 4 for shorter queries).
- For **Protein**: default ktup = **1** or **2**.
- The program builds a **lookup table** mapping each k-tuple → its positions in the query.
- For every word in the database, it checks whether that word appears in the lookup table.
- **Common ktups → diagonals in the dot-matrix** are scored.

#### **Phase 2: Score the Diagonals (residue/diagonal scoring)**
- After locating matching k-tuples, each **diagonal** (offset) in the dot plot is given a score equal to the **number of matching k-tuples** on that diagonal.
- The **highest-scoring diagonals** are selected as candidates (usually top 10 diagonals).

#### **Phase 3: Rescan Top Diagonals with a Substitution Matrix**
- The top scoring diagonals are re-evaluated using a **scoring matrix**:
  - **Proteins**: **BLOSUM50** (default) or **PAM250**.
  - **DNA**: **+5 for match, −4 for mismatch** (or custom).
- A **scoring run** along each diagonal yields a preliminary score called **init1**.

#### **Phase 4: Join Diagonals Using Gaps (initn)**
- Diagonals separated by small gaps (often of size **<16–32 residues**) are **joined** by introducing short gaps.
- The combined score is called **initn** (initial score with allowed gaps).

#### **Phase 5: Banded Smith–Waterman Optimization (opt)**
- Only the region around the highest-scoring **initn** segment is subjected to a **banded Smith–Waterman** dynamic programming alignment.
- The final optimized score is called **opt** and reported as the alignment quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works (the 'Why'):**
> Dynamic programming on a query of length *m* and database of length *n* takes **O(m·n)** time — far too slow for billions of bases. By restricting expensive DP to only the **top-ranked diagonals**, FASTA achieves **O(m·n) in best case → O(m·n/k)** in practice. With ktup=2 and protein DBs of 10⁹ residues, the speedup is enormous.

### 2.2 BLAST — Step-by-Step Algorithm (Three-Phase Model)

#### **Phase 1: Word List Compilation**
- For a query of length *L*, with word size **W** (e.g., **W = 3** for proteins, **W = 11** for DNA), extract all overlapping words of length W.
- There are **(L − W + 1)** such words.
- For each word, the algorithm enumerates **all words scoring above threshold T** when scored against a substitution matrix (BLOSUM62 by default for proteins).
- This generates a **list of "high-scoring words"** (also called *neighborhood words*).

#### **Phase 2: Database Scanning (Seeding)**
- Scan every sequence in the database for **exact occurrences** of any word from the high-scoring word list.
- Each match is called a **seed** or **hit**.
- A hit at position *(i, j)* means: at position *i* of the query and position *j* of the database, a word of score ≥ T was matched.

#### **Phase 3: Un-gapped Extension (HSPC / HSP)**
- Each seed is **extended in both directions** (left and right) without introducing gaps.
- Extension continues as long as the cumulative score increases.
- Extension stops when the score drops below the **maximum score seen so far minus a drop-off X**.
- The resulting **maximal scoring fragment** is called a **High-scoring Segment Pair (HSP)** or **High-scoring Pair (HSP)**.
- The score of the HSP is denoted **S**.
- Only HSPs with **S ≥ S_threshold** (a user-set cutoff, e.g., S ≥ 30 by default for proteins) are reported.

#### **Phase 4 (Optional): Gapped Extension**
- In **gapped BLAST** (the default since 1997), the HSP is used as a **seed for gapped extension**:
  - A small gapped DP is performed around the HSP using **sum statistics** to combine multiple HSPs into a single gapped alignment.
- This further improves sensitivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works (the 'How'):**
> The key insight of BLAST is the **statistical theory of Karlin–Altschul (1990)**: a local alignment score without gaps follows an **Extreme Value Distribution (EVD)**. This allows accurate **E-value** calculation, which is what makes BLAST's results statistically meaningful — not just a number, but a *probability statement*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations for KTU 2024 Board Exams**

| Symbol | Formula / Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **E-value (E)** | $E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$ | $K, \lambda$ from Karlin–Altschul; $m,n$ = effective lengths; $S$ = raw score |
| **Bit score (S′)** | $S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$ | Unitless, normalized, comparable across databases |
| **Bit score from E-value** | $S' = \log_2 \left( \frac{m \cdot n}{E} \right)$ | Useful inverse relation |
| **P-value (P)** | $P = 1 - e^{-E}$ | For small $E$, $P \approx E$ |
| **HSP length expectation** | $\ell \approx \frac{\ln(K m n)}{\lambda}$ | Avg. expected length of HSP |
| **FASTA ktup** | $k = 2 \text{ (protein)}, \quad k = 6 \text{ (DNA)}$ | Default; smaller $k$ = more sensitive |
| **BLAST word size W** | $W = 3 \text{ (protein)}, \quad W = 11 \text{ (DNA)}$ | Default; larger $W$ = faster |
| **Number of query words** | $N_w = L - W + 1$ | $L$ = query length |
| **Karlin λ, K** | Database-dependent | E.g., BLOSUM62: $\lambda \approx 0.267$, $K \approx 0.041$ |
| **Threshold T** | $T = f(\text{composition}, \text{matrix})$ | Word score cutoff in BLAST phase 1 |
| **Score (raw)** | $S = \sum_i s(a_i, b_i)$ | Sum of substitution scores |
| **Identity %** | $\frac{\text{matches}}{\text{aligned length}} \times 100\%$ | Often confused with score |
| **Gap penalty (open/extend)** | $W_{\text{gap}} = g_o + (\ell - 1) \cdot g_e$ | Linear / affine gap cost |

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER confuse E-value with P-value:**
> - **E-value** = expected number of hits with score ≥ S by chance alone. **E < 0.01** is considered significant.
> - **P-value** = probability of seeing ≥ one such hit by chance.
> - For very small E: **P ≈ E**.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of FASTA & BLAST in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> - **Drug Discovery:** Identify homologous proteins of a known drug target across species → faster lead optimization.
> - **Personalized Medicine:** Clinical genomics pipelines (e.g., **BWA + BLAST** variant validation) for diagnosing rare diseases.
> - **Synthetic Biology:** Confirming novel gene designs don't accidentally match viral / hazardous sequences (biosafety screening).
> - **Forensics & Biosecurity:** Detect engineered DNA in synthesized genetic material using BLAST against the **NIH Pathogen Database**.
> - **Metagenomics:** Taxonomic classification of millions of reads via **BLAST against NCBI nt/nr**.
> - **Vaccine Design:** Finding conserved epitopes across strains by aligning protein sequences.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Karlin–Altschul E-value Formula

The **E-value** is the cornerstone of BLAST statistics. We derive it below.

#### Step 1: Setup of the Local Alignment Score
Consider two random sequences of lengths **m** and **n**, with each residue pair contributing a score drawn independently from a substitution matrix. By the theory of **renewal processes**, the optimal local alignment score $S$ follows an **Extreme Value Distribution (Gumbel distribution)**.

#### Step 2: Probability Density Function of S
The probability that a single local alignment achieves score ≥ *x* is given by:

$$
P(S \geq x) = 1 - e^{-K m n \, e^{-\lambda x}}
$$

where:
- $\lambda > 0$ is a scaling parameter (depends on scoring matrix and gap costs).
- $K > 0$ is a search-space scaling parameter (depends on alphabet and scoring system).

#### Step 3: Expected Number of HSPs (E-value)
The **E-value**, denoted $E$, is defined as the **expected number of distinct HSPs** whose score is at least $S$, occurring by chance alone between a query of length $m$ and database of total effective length $n$:

$$
E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}
$$

**Interpretation:** If $E = 1$, we expect one such high-scoring match to occur by chance. If $E = 0.001$, only 1 in 1000 random alignments would produce such a score.

#### Step 4: Relating E-value to Bit Score
The **bit score $S'$** is defined such that:

$$
S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}
$$

This is obtained by inverting the E-value formula and taking base-2 logarithm:

$$
E = K m n \cdot e^{-\lambda S} \;\Longrightarrow\; e^{-\lambda S} = \frac{E}{K m n}
$$

Taking natural log:

$$
-\lambda S = \ln E - \ln(K m n) \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda S = \ln(K m n) - \ln E
$$

So:

$$
S = \frac{\ln(K m n) - \ln E}{\lambda}
$$

To convert to **bits** (unit of mutual information, where each doubling of search space adds 1 bit), divide by $\ln 2$:

$$
S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}
$$

Substituting $S$ from above:

$$
S' = \frac{\ln(K m n) - \ln E - \ln K}{\ln 2} = \frac{\ln(m n) - \ln E}{\ln 2} = \log_2\left(\frac{m n}{E}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
S' = \log_2\left(\frac{m \cdot n}{E}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most important formula for KTU 2024 numerical problems.**
> Given any two of $\{S', E, m, n\}$, the third can be computed.

#### Step 5: P-value from E-value
The probability of seeing **at least one** spurious match with E-value *E* follows the Poisson distribution:

$$
P = 1 - e^{-E}
$$

For **small $E$** (which is the biologically meaningful regime, e.g., $E < 0.05$):

$$
P \approx E
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A BLAST search returns an HSP with raw score $S = 70$. For the BLOSUM62 matrix, $\lambda = 0.267$ and $K = 0.041$. The effective lengths are $m = 250$ and $n = 4.5 \times 10^{10}$. Calculate:
> (a) the E-value
> (b) the Bit score
> (c) the P-value
> (d) State whether the match is statistically significant.

**Solution:**

**(a) E-value:**

$$
E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
E = (0.041) \cdot (250) \cdot (4.5 \times 10^{10}) \cdot e^{-(0.267)(70)}
$$

Compute the exponential first:

$$
\lambda S = 0.267 \times 70 = 18.69
$$

$$
e^{-18.69} \approx 7.45 \times 10^{-9}
$$

Now compute the product:

$$
K \cdot m \cdot n = 0.041 \times 250 \times 4.5 \times 10^{10} = 4.6125 \times 10^{11}
$$

So:

$$
E = (4.6125 \times 10^{11}) \times (7.45 \times 10^{-9}) = 3{,}436.3
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 3{,}436}
$$

**(b) Bit Score:**

$$
S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} = \frac{18.69 - \ln(0.041)}{0.6931}
$$

Compute $\ln(0.041) = -3.194$:

$$
S' = \frac{18.69 - (-3.194)}{0.6931} = \frac{21.884}{0.6931} \approx 31.6
$$

$$
\boxed{S' \approx 32 \text{ bits}}
$$

**(c) P-value:**

$$
P = 1 - e^{-E} = 1 - e^{-3436.3} \approx 1.0
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 1.0 \text{ (essentially certain — by chance)}}
$$

**(d) Significance:**

Since $E = 3436 \gg 0.01$, the match is **NOT statistically significant**. It is most likely a chance occurrence. **No biological conclusion should be drawn.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume "high score = significance". This is FALSE. A score of 70 with $\lambda = 0.267$ may be highly significant in a small database (e.g., $n = 10^6$) and completely insignificant in a large one (e.g., $n = 10^{11}$). **Always compute the E-value.**

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: FASTA vs BLAST

| Criterion | FASTA | BLAST |
|---|---|---|
| **Algorithm** | k-tuple lookup + banded DP | Word lookup + ungapped extension (and optional gapped extension) |
| **Sensitivity (low similarity)** | Slightly higher for divergent sequences | Slightly lower (default) |
| **Speed** | Slower (uses banded DP) | Faster (only extends seeds) |
| **ktup / W** | k = 1–6 (smaller) | W = 3–11 (larger) |
| **Output statistics** | init1, initn, opt | Bit score, E-value, identity % |
| **EVD-based stats** | Optional (via PRSS) | Built-in |
| **Best use case** | Divergent homolog detection | Rapid screening, large DBs |
| **Variants** | FASTA, TFASTA, FASTX/Y, SSEARCH | BLASTN/P/X, PSI-BLAST, PHI-BLAST, MegaBLAST |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FASTA Algorithm — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Query Sequence Q and Database D] --> B[Phase 1: Build k-tuple lookup table from Q]
    B --> C[Scan D for matching k-tuples]
    C --> D[Phase 2: Score diagonals by # matching k-tuples]
    D --> E[Retain top-N diagonals]
    E --> F[Phase 3: Rescan diagonals using scoring matrix BLOSUM50 or PAM250]
    F --> G[Compute init1 score for each diagonal]
    G --> H[Phase 4: Join nearby diagonals with short gaps]
    H --> I[Compute initn score]
    I --> J[Phase 5: Banded Smith-Waterman DP on best initn region]
    J --> K[Compute final opt score]
    K --> L[Report alignments ranked by opt score]
    L --> M[End]
```

### 4.2 BLAST Algorithm — Three-Phase Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Query Sequence Q of length L] --> B[Phase 1: Extract all words of length W]
    B --> C[Enumerate high-scoring words above threshold T]
    C --> D[Phase 2: Scan database for exact matches of high-scoring words]
    D --> E[Mark seed positions]
    E --> F[Phase 3: Extend each seed in both directions]
    F --> G{Score increases?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Continue extension]
    G -- No, drops below cutoff --> I[Stop extension]
    H --> G
    I --> J[Record HSP with score S]
    J --> K{Multiple HSPs for same DB hit?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Phase 4: Gapped extension using sum statistics]
    K -- No --> N[Report single HSP]
    L --> M[Merge HSPs into gapped alignment]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute Bit score and E-value via Karlin-Altschul]
    O --> P[Report ranked list of hits]
    P --> Q[End]
```

### 4.3 BLAST Variants — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User input: Query type and DB type] --> B{Query nucleotide?}
    B -- Yes --> C{DB nucleotide?}
    B -- No --> D{DB protein?}
    C -- Yes --> E[BLASTN: DNA vs DNA]
    C -- No --> F[TBLASTX: Translated DNA vs Translated DNA]
    D -- Yes --> G[BLASTX: Translated DNA vs Protein]
    D -- No --> H[BLASTP: Protein vs Protein]
    A2[Or: Query protein] --> D
    A3[Or: Query translated DNA in 6 frames] --> F
    F --> I[Output: 6-frame translations aligned]
```

### 4.4 Statistical Interpretation Map — E-value Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute E-value from BLAST output] --> B{E < 0.01?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Highly significant match]
    C --> D[Strong evidence of homology]
    B -- No, 0.01 less than E less than 0.05 --> E[Borderline significance]
    E --> F[Investigate further with PSI-BLAST or synteny]
    B -- No, E greater than or equal to 0.05 --> G[Not significant]
    G --> H[Likely chance match]
    H --> I[Do not infer homology]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The threshold **E < 0.01** is the **de-facto standard**, but in practice:
> - **E < 1e-50**: nearly identical proteins / same gene
> - **1e-50 < E < 1e-10**: strong homologs, same family
> - **1e-10 < E < 1e-3**: distant homologs, same superfamily
> - **1e-3 < E < 1**: "twilight zone" — caution required
> - **E > 1**: almost certainly not homologous

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
> Define FASTA. List its main algorithmic phases and the role of the **ktup** parameter.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **FASTA** is a heuristic sequence alignment tool developed by Lipman and Pearson (1985) that compares a query sequence against a database using a **k-tuple (ktup) word-matching strategy** followed by banded Smith–Waterman optimization. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Main phases:** **(2 marks)**
> 1. **Phase 1** — Identify common k-tuples between query and database (word lookup).
> 2. **Phase 2** — Score diagonals based on the number of matching k-tuples; select top diagonals.
> 3. **Phase 3** — Re-score top diagonals using a substitution matrix (e.g., BLOSUM50); compute `init1`.
> 4. **Phase 4** — Join diagonals separated by small gaps to compute `initn`.
> 5. **Phase 5** — Apply banded Smith–Waterman DP on best region to compute `opt` score.
>
> **Role of ktup:** ktup is the word length used in Phase 1. Smaller ktup (e.g., 1–2 for proteins) increases sensitivity (finds weaker similarities) but reduces speed; larger ktup (e.g., 6 for DNA) increases speed at the cost of sensitivity.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> What is the **E-value** in BLAST? Write the Karlin–Altschul equation and explain each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The **E-value (Expect value)** is the **number of expected alignments with a score ≥ S that would occur by chance alone** when searching a database of the given size. A smaller E-value indicates greater statistical significance. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Karlin–Altschul Equation:** **(2 marks)**
>
> $$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$$
>
> Where:
> - $K$ = search-space scaling constant (depends on scoring matrix and alphabet).
> - $\lambda$ = score-scaling constant (depends on scoring system).
> - $m$ = effective length of the query sequence.
> - $n$ = total effective length of the database.
> - $S$ = raw alignment score.
>
> **Significance threshold:** $E < 0.01$ is generally considered significant.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three-phase algorithm of BLAST** with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of the **word size W** and the **threshold T** in determining sensitivity and speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BLAST search of a protein query of length $m = 320$ against a database of effective length $n = 1.2 \times 10^{10}$ yields an HSP with raw score $S = 95$. For the BLOSUM62 matrix, $\lambda = 0.267$ and $K = 0.041$. Compute the **E-value**, the **Bit score**, and comment on the **statistical significance** of the match. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]:**

> [!NOTE]
> **BLAST Algorithm Phases:**
>
> **Phase 1 – Word list compilation:** From a query of length $L$, extract all overlapping words of length $W$ (default $W = 3$ for proteins). For each such word, enumerate **all words scoring ≥ T** against the substitution matrix (BLOSUM62). The collection is called the **high-scoring word list**. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> **Phase 2 – Scanning (Seeding):** Scan every database sequence for **exact matches** of any word from the high-scoring word list. Each match (query position $i$, DB position $j$) is recorded as a **seed (hit)**. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> **Phase 3 – Un-gapped Extension (HSP):** Each seed is extended in both directions without gaps. The cumulative score is tracked; extension stops when the score drops below the **maximum-so-far minus drop-off X**. The final maximal scoring fragment is a **High-scoring Segment Pair (HSP)** with score $S$. Only HSPs with $S \geq S_{\text{threshold}}$ are kept. **(2 marks)**
>
> **Optional Phase 4 – Gapped extension:** For multiple HSPs on the same diagonal, gapped DP is performed using **sum statistics (Karlin–Altschul 1993)**. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Role of W and T:** **(1 mark)**
> - **W (word size):** Larger $W$ → fewer seeds → faster but less sensitive. Smaller $W$ → more seeds → slower but more sensitive. Typical values: W = 3 (proteins), W = 11 (DNA).
> - **T (threshold):** Higher T → only highly conserved words qualify → fewer hits, faster. Lower T → more permissive words → more hits, slower.

**Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1: Compute $\lambda S$:**
>
> $$\lambda S = 0.267 \times 95 = 25.365$$
>
> **(0.5 mark)**
>
> **Step 2: Compute $e^{-\lambda S}$:**
>
> $$e^{-25.365} \approx 9.97 \times 10^{-12}$$
>
> **(0.5 mark)**
>
> **Step 3: Compute $K \cdot m \cdot n$:**
>
> $$K \cdot m \cdot n = 0.041 \times 320 \times 1.2 \times 10^{10} = 1.5744 \times 10^{11}$$
>
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 4: Compute the E-value:**
>
> $$E = (1.5744 \times 10^{11}) \times (9.97 \times 10^{-12}) \approx 1.57$$
>
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 5: Compute the Bit score:**
>
> $$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} = \frac{25.365 - \ln(0.041)}{0.6931}$$
>
> $$\ln(0.041) = -3.194$$
>
> $$S' = \frac{25.365 + 3.194}{0.6931} = \frac{28.559}{0.6931} \approx 41.2$$
>
> **(2 marks)**
>
> **Step 6: Interpretation:**
>
> Since $E \approx 1.57 > 0.01$, the match is **borderline / not statistically significant by the strict criterion**. However, $S' \approx 41$ bits is a moderately strong score and may indicate **distant homology**. The student should recommend running **PSI-BLAST** iteration or checking **domain-level matches**. **(2 marks)**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **FASTA algorithm in detail**, comparing it with BLAST. Mention the significance of the **`init1`, `initn`, and `opt` scores** in FASTA output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A researcher performs a **BLASTN** search with a 600 bp DNA query against the NCBI nt database (effective length $n = 8 \times 10^{13}$ bp). For the default nucleotide scoring (match = +5, mismatch = −4) with $\lambda = 1.39$ and $K = 0.75$, an HSP of length 180 bp is reported with **150 matches** and **30 mismatches**. Compute the **raw score S, the E-value, and the Bit score**. Is this hit significant? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]:**

> [!NOTE]
> **FASTA Algorithm:** **(5 marks)**
>
> 1. **Phase 1 – k-tuple lookup:** Decompose query into overlapping words of length `ktup` (default 6 for DNA, 2 for protein). Build a position-indexed lookup table.
> 2. **Phase 2 – Diagonal scoring:** Find all matching k-tuples in the database; count matches per diagonal; keep top diagonals.
> 3. **Phase 3 – Substitution matrix rescan:** Re-score top diagonals using BLOSUM50 / PAM250 (proteins) or match/mismatch (DNA); obtain **`init1`**.
> 4. **Phase 4 – Diagonal joining with gaps:** Combine diagonals separated by small gaps; obtain **`initn`**.
> 5. **Phase 5 – Banded Smith–Waterman DP:** Optimize the best `initn` region to obtain **`opt`** — the final reported score.
>
> **Score Interpretations:** **(1 mark)**
> - **`init1`**: preliminary diagonal score after matrix rescoring.
> - **`initn`**: score after joining diagonals with gaps.
> - **`opt`**: final optimal alignment score (most reliable).
>
> **FASTA vs BLAST comparison:** **(1 mark)** FASTA uses banded DP (slower but more sensitive); BLAST uses pure extension (faster, slight loss in sensitivity for very divergent sequences). FASTA outputs `init1/initn/opt`; BLAST outputs **Bit score and E-value**.

**Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1: Compute raw score S:**
> Each match = +5, each mismatch = −4.
>
> $$S = (150)(+5) + (30)(-4) = 750 - 120 = 630$$
>
> **(1.5 marks)**
>
> **Step 2: Compute $\lambda S$:**
>
> $$\lambda S = 1.39 \times 630 = 875.7$$
>
> **(0.5 mark)**
>
> **Step 3: Compute $e^{-\lambda S}$:**
>
> $$e^{-875.7} \approx 0 \quad (\text{far below machine precision, } < 10^{-300})$$
>
> **(0.5 mark)**
>
> **Step 4: Compute $K \cdot m \cdot n$:** (use $m \approx L = 600$)
>
> $$K \cdot m \cdot n = 0.75 \times 600 \times 8 \times 10^{13} = 3.6 \times 10^{16}$$
>
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 5: Compute E-value:**
>
> $$E = (3.6 \times 10^{16}) \times (\text{essentially zero}) \approx 0$$
>
> Numerically, $E < 10^{-300}$, effectively zero. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 6: Compute Bit score:**
>
> $$S' = \frac{875.7 - \ln(0.75)}{0.6931} = \frac{875.7 + 0.2877}{0.6931} \approx 1263.7 \text{ bits}$$
>
> **(1.5 marks)**
>
> **Step 7: Conclusion:** $E \approx 0 \ll 0.01$, hence the match is **extremely highly significant**. The 600-bp query has an exact or near-exact match (150/180 = 83.3% identity over 180 bp) in nt. This is almost certainly a **true homolog** or **same gene**. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU 2024 FASTA/BLAST Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up FASTA's ktup with BLAST's W:** FASTA uses small ktup (2/6); BLAST uses large W (3/11). Examiners specifically test this. **−2 marks if confused.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert raw score to bit score:** Many students report only the raw score. KTU expects **both E-value AND bit score**. **−3 marks if either is missing.**
>
> 3. **Not stating significance criteria:** Saying "E = 1.57, so the hit is significant" is **WRONG**. Always state: *"$E < 0.01$ → significant; $E \geq 0.01$ → not significant."* **−2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Confusing P-value with E-value:** E = expected number; P = probability of ≥1. **−1 mark** if swapped.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in formulae:** $m, n$ are **effective lengths** (after composition-based adjustment), not raw lengths. Most questions use raw lengths as approximation — but **state this assumption** to be safe. **−1 mark.**
>
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions:** KTU requires a flowchart or block diagram for algorithm explanations. A neat diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** alone. **−2 marks** if missing.
>
> 7. **Writing Smith–Waterman instead of Banded Smith–Waterman** for FASTA Phase 5: KTU specifically tests the **banded** nature (only a band of width ~32 along the diagonal is computed). **−1 mark.**
>
> 8. **Omitting the Karlin–Altschul statistical basis** for BLAST's E-value: Examiners love this concept. A student who says "E-value is the expected number of chance hits" without mentioning **Karlin–Altschul / Gumbel distribution** loses marks. **−2 marks.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Exam**

- [x] **FASTA** = Lipman & Pearson 1985; uses **k-tuple lookup + banded DP**; outputs `init1 / initn / opt`.
- [x] **BLAST** = Altschul et al. 1990; uses **word lookup + extension**; outputs **Bit score + E-value**.
- [x] **ktup** (FASTA) is **small** (2 / 6); **W** (BLAST) is **large** (3 / 11) — opposite by design.
- [x] **HSP (High-scoring Segment Pair)** is the unit of output in BLAST.
- [x] **E-value equation:** $E = K m n e^{-\lambda S}$ — memorize cold.
- [x] **Bit score equation:** $S' = (\lambda S - \ln K) / \ln 2 = \log_2(mn / E)$ — alternate form.
- [x] **P-value:** $P = 1 - e^{-E}$; for small $E$, $P \approx E$.
- [x] **Significance rule:** $E < 0.01 \Rightarrow$ significant; $E < 1e\text{-}10 \Rightarrow$ strong; $E < 1e\text{-}50 \Rightarrow$ nearly identical.
- [x] **Bit scores are comparable across databases; raw scores are NOT.**
- [x] **BLAST variants:**
  - **BLASTN** = DNA vs DNA
  - **BLASTP** = protein vs protein
  - **BLASTX** = translated DNA (6 frames) vs protein
  - **TBLASTN** = protein vs translated DNA (6 frames)
  - **TBLASTX** = translated DNA vs translated DNA (6 frames × 6 frames)
- [x] **PSI-BLAST** = Position-Specific Iterated BLAST — builds a PSSM for sensitive remote homology detection.
- [x] **PHI-BLAST** = Pattern-Hit Initiated BLAST — uses a conserved motif to anchor the search.
- [x] **FASTA variants:** FASTA, TFASTA, FASTX (DNA→protein vs protein DB), FASTY (DNA→protein vs protein DB, full Smith–Waterman), SSEARCH (full Smith–Waterman).
- [x] **Karlin–Altschul statistics** assume **ungapped alignments**; for gapped BLAST, modified parameters apply (still EVD-based).
- [x] **Twilight zone:** $E \approx 1$ to $10^{-3}$ — common for divergent proteins; use structure-based methods.
- [x] **NCBI BLAST URL (KTU practical reference):** `https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/`
- [x] **Default scoring matrices:** Proteins → **BLOSUM62**; DNA → match/mismatch (+5/−4 or +2/−3).
- [x] **Time complexity:**
  - Smith–Waterman (full DP): $O(mn)$
  - BLAST: ~$O(m + n)$ with $O(1)$ extensions per seed
  - FASTA: ~$O(m + n + \text{band DP cost})$
- [x] **Three-phase BLAST** = Word list → Seeding (DB scan) → Extension (HSP generation).
- [x] **Karlin–Altschul parameters** (BLOSUM62 typical): $\lambda \approx 0.267$, $K \approx 0.041$, $H \approx 0.14$.
- [x] **MegaBLAST** = faster variant for nearly identical sequences (W = 28 for DNA).
- [x] **Discontiguous MegaBLAST** = handles cross-species comparisons better (uses codon-style patterns).
- [x] **Masking:** Low-complexity regions (e.g., `AAAAA...`, `QQQQQ...`) should be masked (lowercase) to avoid spurious hits — done via **SEG** (proteins) or **DUST** (DNA) filters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Sequence Alignments: Common Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT615 - BIOINFORMATICS TECHNIQUES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple Sequence Alignments: Common Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA)** is the computational procedure of arranging three or more biological sequences (DNA, RNA, or protein) of varying lengths into a unified matrix by inserting gaps (`-`) such that residues/ nucleotides in the same column are placed in homologous positions, maximizing a defined **objective scoring function** that reflects evolutionary, structural, or functional similarity.

Mathematically, an MSA of $N$ sequences of lengths $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_N$ produces an $N \times L$ matrix (where $L \geq \max(L_i)$) such that:

$$S_{MSA} = \arg\max_{A} \; \mathcal{F}(A)$$

where $A$ denotes one possible alignment and $\mathcal{F}$ is the chosen scoring objective (Sum-of-Pairs SP-score, entropy-based score, log-likelihood, etc.).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Class Photo" Intuition

Imagine you are a school photographer arranging **5 students of different heights** into a single row for a class photo. The constraint? Their **eyes must line up horizontally** (the most meaningful feature), even if it means placing short students on stilts (gaps) or asking tall students to bend slightly.

- The **horizontal rows of eyes** = aligned columns of homologous residues.
- The **stilts** = insertion gaps (`-`).
- The **"best photo"** = the alignment with the highest similarity score.

Just as a perfect eye-level lineup requires a measure of "how aligned are the eyes", an MSA requires a numerical score quantifying column-wise similarity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is MSA harder than Pairwise Alignment?**
> Pairwise alignment has a single optimal substructure (dynamic programming on a 2D grid). MSA operates in an $N$-dimensional lattice. For $N$ sequences of average length $L$, exact DP requires $O(L^N)$ time and memory — computationally intractable for $N \geq 4$.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **SP-Score (Sum-of-Pairs)** — **most common objective**; sum of pairwise scores across all induced pairwise alignments.
- **COFFEE Score** — consistency-based objective.
- **GOP / GEP** — Gap Open Penalty and Gap Extension Penalty (default scoring parameters).
- **Guide Tree** — a binary dendrogram used to dictate the order of progressive alignment merges.
- **Substitution Matrix** — BLOSUM62, PAM250 (proteins); identity matrix (DNA).
- **PSSM (Position-Specific Scoring Matrix)** — profile used in profile-profile alignment and HMM-based MSA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Keyword — "Progressive Alignment"**: Defined by Feng & Doolittle (1987), this is the dominant heuristic for MSA. It builds a **guide tree** from pairwise distances, then progressively aligns the most similar pair, then next-most-similar, etc. — greedily producing a final MSA. ClustalW, Clustal Omega, Kalign, and MUSCLE (partially) are progressive implementations.

---

## 1.4 Classification of MSA Methods (Overview)

| Class | Representative Tool | Time Complexity (approx.) |
|---|---|---|
| **Progressive** | ClustalW, Kalign | $O(N^2 L^2) + O(N^3)$ |
| **Iterative Refinement** | MUSCLE, PRRN | $O(N^3 L) \text{ to } O(N^4 L)$ |
| **HMM / Probabilistic** | HMMER, SAM, MAFFT (part) | $O(N L^2)$ |
| **Consistency-based** | T-Coffee, ProbCons | $O(N^2 L^2)$ |
| **Exact (DP)** | MSA (Gupta), partial in DIALIGN | $O(L^N)$ — exponential |
| **Anchor / Segment-based** | MAFFT (FFT-mode), DIALIGN | $O(N L \log L)$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MSA Scoring Problem — Formal Decomposition

An MSA is scored by summing contributions per column. The dominant approach is the **Sum-of-Pairs (SP)** objective.

### 2.1.1 SP-Score Definition

For an MSA of $N$ sequences and $L$ aligned columns, the SP-score is:

$$S_{SP} = \sum_{k=1}^{L} \sum_{1 \le i < j \le N} s(x_i^k, x_j^k)$$

where $x_i^k$ is the residue (or gap `-`) of sequence $i$ in column $k$, and $s(\cdot,\cdot)$ is a **pairwise substitution score** (e.g., from BLOSUM62).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SP-Score?** It is **decomposable** by column, allowing each column to be scored independently. This permits the efficient **profile alignment** mechanism used by ClustalW (you align two pre-aligned profiles as if they were single sequences).

### 2.1.2 Why SP-Score Has a "Bias"

SP-score has been criticized because it treats every pair with equal weight and over-counts redundant information in closely-related sequences. Corrected variants exist:

- **Weighted SP-Score (WSP):**
$$S_{WSP} = \sum_{k=1}^{L} \sum_{i<j} w_{ij} \cdot s(x_i^k, x_j^k)$$

  where $w_{ij}$ is a tree-derived weight (used in ClustalW and MUSCLE).

- **Column Score (Entropy-based):**
$$H(k) = - \sum_{a \in \Sigma} p_a^k \log_2 p_a^k$$

  Lower entropy → more conserved column → better alignment.

---

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Any MSA Method

### Pillar 1 — Distance Estimation Between Sequences
Pairwise distances are estimated via:
- **Identity-based:** $d_{ij} = 1 - \frac{\text{identities}}{\text{aligned length}}$
- **Substitution-score-based:** $d_{ij} = -\ln\left(\frac{S_{ij} - S_{rand}}{S_{max} - S_{rand}}\right)$ (Kimura-like correction)
- **k-mer distance** (MAFFT): count shared $k$-tuples for speed.

### Pillar 2 — Guide Tree Construction
A **rooted binary tree** is built from the $N \times N$ distance matrix using:
- **UPGMA** (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean) — assumes a molecular clock.
- **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** — Saitou & Nei (1987); corrects for rate heterogeneity.

NJ algorithm formula for corrected distance:

$$d_{ij}^* = d_{ij} - (r_i + r_j)$$

where $r_i = \frac{1}{N-2}\sum_{k \ne i} d_{ik}$.

### Pillar 3 — Progressive Alignment
Traverse the guide tree from leaves to root, aligning pairs (or profiles) at each internal node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Once a gap is introduced in progressive alignment, it is never removed.** This is the **"gotcha"** of the progressive method — early mistakes propagate. Iterative refinement methods try to fix this by partitioning and re-aligning.

---

## 2.3 Gap Penalty Models

| Model | Formula | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $g = g_o \cdot \ell$ | Simple DP |
| **Affine** | $g = g_o + g_e \cdot (\ell - 1)$ | ClustalW (default) |
| **Logarithmic** | $g = g_o + g_e \cdot \ln(\ell)$ | Long-gap tolerant tools |
| **Position-specific** | $g_{ij} = g_o(j) + g_e(j) \cdot \ell$ | Profile alignment |

Here $\ell$ = gap length; $g_o$ = **Gap Open Penalty**; $g_e$ = **Gap Extension Penalty**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip**: Memorize the SP-score formula, the NJ corrected distance formula, and the affine gap penalty. These three are the most-asked formulas in KTU Module 2 derivations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SP-Score | $S_{SP} = \sum_{k=1}^{L}\sum_{i<j} s(x_i^k, x_j^k)$ | Unitless (log-odds bits) |
| 2 | Weighted SP-Score | $S_{WSP} = \sum_{k=1}^{L}\sum_{i<j} w_{ij}\,s(x_i^k, x_j^k)$ | $w_{ij} \in (0,1]$ |
| 3 | Column Entropy | $H(k) = -\sum_a p_a^k \log_2 p_a^k$ | Bits; lower = more conserved |
| 4 | Pairwise Distance | $d_{ij} = 1 - \frac{ID_{ij}}{L_{ij}}$ | $0 \le d_{ij} \le 1$ |
| 5 | Kimura Corrected Distance | $d_{ij} = -\ln(1 - p - 0.2p^2)$ | $p$ = observed mismatches |
| 6 | NJ Rate Term | $r_i = \frac{1}{N-2}\sum_{k\ne i}d_{ik}$ | For NJ correction |
| 7 | NJ Corrected Distance | $d_{ij}^{*} = d_{ij} - (r_i + r_j)$ | Used in NJ join criterion |
| 8 | Affine Gap Penalty | $g(\ell) = g_o + g_e(\ell - 1)$ | $\ell \ge 1$ |
| 9 | Profile Profile Score | $S_{PQ}(a,b) = \sum_{a,b} f_a^P f_b^Q s(a,b)$ | $f_a^P$ = frequency in profile $P$ |
| 10 | Log-Likelihood (HMM) | $\log P(\text{seqs} \mid M) = \sum_k \log P(x^k \mid M)$ | Used in HMMER, SAM |

---

## 2.5 Engineering/Bioinformatics Utility

- **Phylogenetics**: MSAs are the prerequisite for nearly every downstream phylogenetic tree.
- **Motif/Pattern Discovery**: Conserved columns reveal functional motifs (active sites, signal peptides).
- **Structure Prediction**: Homology modelling (Swiss-Model, AlphaFold-multimer) consumes MSAs as input.
- **Drug Target Identification**: Conserved residues across pathogen strains pinpoint essential regions.
- **Primer Design**: Conserved regions are targeted by universal PCR primers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Computing SP-Score Manually

**Setup:** Three aligned sequences, BLOSUM62 substitution matrix used:

```
Seq1:  A  R  -  G
Seq2:  A  K  N  G
Seq3:  S  R  N  -
```

Column-wise residue list:
- Column 1: `{A, A, S}`
- Column 2: `{R, K, R}`
- Column 3: `{-, N, N}`
- Column 4: `{G, G, -}`

### Step 1 — Retrieve Pairwise Scores from BLOSUM62 (sample values used)

$$s(A,A) = 4, \quad s(A,S) = 1, \quad s(S,A) = 1$$
$$s(R,K) = 3, \quad s(R,R) = 5, \quad s(K,R) = 3$$
$$s(N,N) = 6, \quad s(-,N) = -8, \quad s(N,-) = -8$$
$$s(G,G) = 6, \quad s(G,-) = -8, \quad s(-,G) = -8$$

### Step 2 — Column 1 Contribution

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= s(A,A) + s(A,S) + s(A,S) \\
&= 4 + 1 + 1 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Column 2 Contribution

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2 &= s(R,K) + s(R,R) + s(K,R) \\
&= 3 + 5 + 3 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Column 3 Contribution

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_3 &= s(-,N) + s(-,N) + s(N,N) \\
&= (-8) + (-8) + 6 = -10
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Column 4 Contribution

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_4 &= s(G,G) + s(G,-) + s(G,-) \\
&= 6 + (-8) + (-8) = -10
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Total SP-Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{SP} &= C_1 + C_2 + C_3 + C_4 \\
&= 6 + 11 + (-10) + (-10) \\
&= -3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The negative score shows that the alignment is penalized mainly by the gap-residue mismatches in columns 3 and 4. A better alignment would shift the gaps to a single column to reduce penalty events.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Neighbor-Joining (NJ) Join Criterion

Given an $N \times N$ symmetric distance matrix $D = [d_{ij}]$, NJ picks the pair $(i,j)$ that minimizes the **Q-criterion**:

$$Q(i,j) = (N-2)\,d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$$

where $r_i = \sum_{k \ne i} d_{ik}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Start:** Define the total branch length of a star tree with center node:

$$S_{star} = \sum_{i<j} d_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_i r_i$$

**Branch lengths to hypothetical neighbors** of $i$ and $j$ (after joining):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(i,u) &= \frac{1}{2}\,d_{ij} + \frac{1}{2(N-2)}(r_i - r_j) \\
\delta(j,u) &= \frac{1}{2}\,d_{ij} + \frac{1}{2(N-2)}(r_j - r_i) \\
\delta(k,u) &= \frac{1}{2}\,(d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij}), \quad k \ne i,j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of $Q(i,j)$:** Subtract the total star-tree length from the length of the tree with a new internal node $u$ connecting $i,j$. After algebraic manipulation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(i,j) &= \sum_{k \ne i,j} d_{ik} + \sum_{k \ne i,j} d_{jk} + (N-2)\,d_{ij} - \sum_k d_{ik} - \sum_k d_{jk} \\
&= (N-2)\,d_{ij} - r_i - r_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result:** NJ picks the pair minimizing $Q(i,j)$ at each iteration, joins them into a new compound node, recomputes distances to all others as:

$$
d_{u,k} = \frac{1}{2}\bigl(d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij}\bigr), \quad k \ne i,j
$$

and repeats until $N - 1$ joins produce the full binary tree.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — UPGMA on 4 Sequences

**Distance Matrix (initial):**

$$
D_0 = \begin{bmatrix}
- & 0.20 & 0.50 & 0.40 \\
0.20 & - & 0.45 & 0.55 \\
0.50 & 0.45 & - & 0.30 \\
0.40 & 0.55 & 0.30 & -
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 1 — Find Minimum

$$\min(D_0) = d_{12} = 0.20$$

Join Seq1 and Seq2 into cluster $C_{12}$.

### Step 2 — Update Distances (UPGMA Formula)

$$d(C_{12}, k) = \frac{n_1\,d_{1k} + n_2\,d_{2k}}{n_1 + n_2}$$

With $n_1 = n_2 = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_{12}, 3) &= \frac{1 \cdot 0.50 + 1 \cdot 0.45}{2} = 0.475 \\
d(C_{12}, 4) &= \frac{1 \cdot 0.40 + 1 \cdot 0.55}{2} = 0.475
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — New 3x3 Matrix

$$
D_1 = \begin{bmatrix}
- & 0.475 & 0.475 \\
0.475 & - & 0.30 \\
0.475 & 0.30 & -
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Next Join

$$\min(D_1) = d_{34} = 0.30$$

Join Seq3 and Seq4 into $C_{34}$.

### Step 5 — Final Distance

$$
d(C_{12}, C_{34}) = \frac{1\cdot 0.475 + 1\cdot 0.475}{2} = 0.475
$$

### Step 6 — Resulting Tree (Newick Format)

```
((Seq1, Seq2), (Seq3, Seq4));
```

with internal branch heights: $C_{12}$ at 0.10, $C_{34}$ at 0.15, root at 0.475.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common MSA Methods

| Algorithm | Year | Strategy | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ClustalW** | 1994 | Progressive + position-specific gap penalties | Widely used; position-specific gaps | Gap propagation errors |
| **MAFFT** | 2002 | FFT-based + iterative refinement | Fast on long sequences | Sensitive to guide tree |
| **MUSCLE** | 2004 | Iterative refinement (two-stage) | Highest accuracy on benchmarks | Slower than ClustalW |
| **T-Coffee** | 2000 | Consistency-based library | High local accuracy | Memory-heavy for large $N$ |
| **ProbCons** | 2005 | Probabilistic consistency | Highest accuracy (small $N$) | Quadratic in $N$ |
| **Kalign** | 2005 | Wu-Manber string matching | Extremely fast | Lower accuracy on divergent sets |
| **DIALIGN** | 1998 | Segment-based | Good for local/conserved motifs | Slow on long sequences |
| **HMMER / SAM** | 1990s | Profile HMM (Plan 7, SAM-T99) | Statistical rigor | Needs training data |
| **MSAProbs** | 2010 | Bayesian + pair-HMM | State-of-the-art accuracy | Slow for $N > 100$ |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step — ClustalW Algorithm (Feng-Doolittle Progressive)

The canonical ClustalW pipeline has three phases:

### Phase 1 — Pairwise Distance Computation
For all $\binom{N}{2}$ pairs, perform a **global Needleman-Wunsch** alignment and record the alignment score $S_{ij}$. Convert to distance:

$$d_{ij} = 1 - \frac{\text{idents}_{ij}}{L_{ij}}$$

or use the **Kimura correction** $d_{ij} = -\ln(1 - p - p^2/5)$ where $p$ = fractional mismatches.

### Phase 2 — Guide Tree via NJ
Build the NJ tree from $D = [d_{ij}]$. Each leaf is a sequence; each internal node stores a profile.

### Phase 3 — Progressive Alignment
Walk from leaves to root. At each internal node:
1. Take the two child sub-alignments $A_L$ and $A_R$.
2. Compute a **profile** for each (frequency vector per column + gap info).
3. Run a **profile-vs-profile** dynamic programming using **position-specific gap penalties**.
4. The resulting merged alignment is stored at the internal node.

> [!NOTE]
> **Position-Specific Gap Penalty in ClustalW**: Reduces gap opening in already-gapped columns and increases it near existing gaps — preventing scattered single-residue gaps. Formula:
> $$g_{o}^{\text{new}}(k) = g_o^{\text{default}} \cdot \alpha \cdot \beta \cdot \gamma$$
> where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are factors based on local gap density, residue type (hydrophobic/polar), and residue proximity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MSA Pipeline — High-Level Block Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the canonical **Input → Scoring → Output** architecture of a Progressive MSA system (ClustalW/Omega-style).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input N Sequences FASTA"] --> B["Phase 1 Pairwise Alignment N choose 2 pairs"]
    B --> C["Distance Matrix D symmetric NxN"]
    C --> D["Phase 2 Guide Tree NJ or UPGMA"]
    D --> E["Phase 3 Progressive Alignment post order traversal"]
    E --> F["Profile Profile DP at each internal node"]
    F --> G["Merged Sub Alignment stored"]
    G --> H{"More internal nodes"}
    H -- Yes --> E
    H -- No --> I["Final MSA output CLUSTAL or FASTA"]
    F --> J["Position Specific Gap Penalties GOP GEP adjusted"]
    J --> F
```

---

## 4.2 ClustalW Algorithm — Detailed Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Input Sequences"] --> P1A["Sequence Normalization"]
    P1A --> P1B["Pairwise Global NW Alignment"]
    P1B --> P1C["Score to Distance Conversion"]
    P1C --> P2["NxN Distance Matrix"]

    P2 --> P2A["NJ Algorithm Q criterion minimization"]
    P2A --> P2B["Branch Length Estimation"]
    P2B --> P3["Rooted Guide Tree Newick"]

    P3 --> P3A["Postorder Tree Traversal"]
    P3A --> P3B["Profile Extraction at Leaves"]
    P3B --> P3C["Profile Profile DP Affine Gap"]
    P3C --> P3D["Gap Penalty Modulation"]
    P3D --> P3E["Merge Two Profiles"]
    P3E --> P3A

    P3E --> P4["Final MSA Matrix N rows x L cols"]
```

---

## 4.3 Scoring Decision Flow — SP-Score Column Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start Column k of MSA"] --> S1["Enumerate residues a1 a2 ... aN"]
    S1 --> S2["Initialize column score C_k = 0"]
    S2 --> S3["Loop i = 1 to N-1"]
    S3 --> S4["Loop j = i+1 to N"]
    S4 --> S5["Lookup substitution score s ai aj or gap penalty"]
    S5 --> S6["C_k = C_k + w_ij times s"]
    S6 --> S4
    S4 -- Done --> S3
    S3 -- Done --> S7["Store C_k move to k+1"]
    S7 --> S8{"k = L"}
    S8 -- No --> S1
    S8 -- Yes --> S9["S_total = sum over k of C_k"]
```

---

## 4.4 Iterative Refinement Architecture (MUSCLE-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0["Initial MSA from Progressive Pass"] --> M1["Partition alignment into 2 subsets"]
    M1 --> M2["Re align subset A subset B using profile from remainder"]
    M2 --> M3["Compare new SP score vs old SP score"]
    M3 --> M4{"SP score improved"}
    M4 -- Yes --> M5["Accept new alignment"]
    M4 -- No --> M6["Revert to old alignment"]
    M5 --> M7{"Max iterations reached"}
    M6 --> M7
    M7 -- No --> M1
    M7 -- Yes --> M8["Output final refined MSA"]
```

---

## 4.5 Conceptual Decision Tree — Choosing an MSA Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Need to align sequences"] --> Q2{"How many sequences N"}
    Q2 -- "N small le 50" --> Q3{"Need highest accuracy"}
    Q2 -- "N medium 50 to 500" --> Q4["Use MAFFT or MUSCLE"]
    Q2 -- "N large gt 500" --> Q5["Use Kalign or Clustal Omega"]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q6["Use ProbCons or MSAProbs"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q7["Use ClustalW"]
    Q4 --> Q8["Use MAFFT FFT mode for speed"]
    Q5 --> Q9["Use Guide Tree UPGMA scalable"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Style)]**
*Define Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA). What is its significance in bioinformatics analysis?*

**Model Answer:**
Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA) is the process of aligning three or more biological sequences (DNA, RNA, or protein) by inserting gaps such that residues in the same column represent homologous positions, optimizing a scoring function (typically Sum-of-Pairs score).

**Significance:**
1. **Phylogenetic Inference** — provides input for evolutionary tree construction.
2. **Identification of Conserved Motifs** — reveals functionally important regions.
3. **Structure Prediction** — essential input for homology modeling and AlphaFold-multimer.
4. **Primer & Drug Design** — conserved regions are targets for universal primers and broad-spectrum therapeutics.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner expects: definition (1 mark) + any 2 of the above uses (1 mark each).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper Style)]**
*What is a guide tree? Why is it needed in progressive MSA?*

**Model Answer:**
A **guide tree** is a rooted binary tree built from a pairwise distance matrix, used to determine the order in which sequences/profiles are merged during progressive alignment.

**Why needed:**
1. Determines the **alignment order** from most-similar pairs to least-similar pairs — leveraging the observation that aligning similar sequences first reduces error.
2. Provides **tree-based weights** $w_{ij}$ for the weighted SP-score.
3. Acts as a **computational skeleton** that converts an exponential DP problem into $N-1$ tractable pairwise alignments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper Style)]**

(a) *Explain the Sum-of-Pairs (SP) scoring scheme for MSA. Derive its mathematical formulation and compute the SP-score for a sample 3-sequence alignment.* **[7 Marks]**

(b) *Describe the Neighbor-Joining (NJ) algorithm for guide tree construction with the Q-criterion. Show its application on a small 4-sequence distance matrix.* **[7 Marks]**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — SP-Score Derivation + Worked Example

**Definition:** The Sum-of-Pairs score is the column-wise sum of pairwise substitution scores over all $\binom{N}{2}$ pairs.

**Formula:**

$$S_{SP} = \sum_{k=1}^{L} \sum_{1 \le i < j \le N} s(x_i^k, x_j^k)$$

**Derivation:**
1. The MSA induces $L$ aligned columns; each column $k$ contains $N$ residues (one per sequence, possibly a gap).
2. The column score is defined as:

$$C_k = \sum_{i<j} s(x_i^k, x_j^k)$$

3. The total alignment score is decomposable as the sum over columns:

$$S_{SP} = \sum_{k=1}^{L} C_k$$

**Worked Example:** Same as Section 3.1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Alignment:} \quad & \text{Seq1: A R - G} \\
& \text{Seq2: A K N G} \\
& \text{Seq3: S R N -}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{SP} &= \underbrace{6}_{C_1} + \underbrace{11}_{C_2} + \underbrace{(-10)}_{C_3} + \underbrace{(-10)}_{C_4} \\
&= -3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Neighbor-Joining Algorithm

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Start with $N$ nodes (sequences), one per leaf.
2. For each pair $(i,j)$, compute the Q-criterion:

$$Q(i,j) = (N-2)\,d_{ij} - r_i - r_j, \quad r_i = \sum_{k \ne i} d_{ik}$$

3. Select pair $(i,j)$ minimizing $Q(i,j)$ and join them into a new node $u$.
4. Compute distances from $u$ to all other nodes $k$:

$$d_{uk} = \frac{1}{2}\bigl(d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij}\bigr)$$

5. Remove $i$ and $j$ from the active set; add $u$. Set $N := N-1$.
6. Repeat steps 2–5 until $N = 2$; the last two nodes are joined to form the root.

**Worked Example on 4 Sequences (Section 3.3 already done with UPGMA; here NJ variant):**

Distance matrix:

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix}
- & 0.20 & 0.50 & 0.40 \\
0.20 & - & 0.45 & 0.55 \\
0.50 & 0.45 & - & 0.30 \\
0.40 & 0.55 & 0.30 & -
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $r_i = \sum_{k \ne i} d_{ik}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= 0.20 + 0.50 + 0.40 = 1.10 \\
r_2 &= 0.20 + 0.45 + 0.55 = 1.20 \\
r_3 &= 0.50 + 0.45 + 0.30 = 1.25 \\
r_4 &= 0.40 + 0.55 + 0.30 = 1.25
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $Q(i,j)$ with $N=4 \Rightarrow N-2 = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(1,2) &= 2(0.20) - 1.10 - 1.20 = -1.70 \\
Q(1,3) &= 2(0.50) - 1.10 - 1.25 = -0.35 \\
Q(1,4) &= 2(0.40) - 1.10 - 1.25 = -0.55 \\
Q(2,3) &= 2(0.45) - 1.20 - 1.25 = -0.55 \\
Q(2,4) &= 2(0.55) - 1.20 - 1.25 = -0.35 \\
Q(3,4) &= 2(0.30) - 1.25 - 1.25 = -0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum:** $Q(1,2) = -1.70$ — join Seq1 & Seq2 first.

$$
d_{u,3} = 0.5 \times (0.50 + 0.45 - 0.20) = 0.375
$$
$$
d_{u,4} = 0.5 \times (0.40 + 0.55 - 0.20) = 0.375
$$

New matrix (3 nodes: $u$, Seq3, Seq4):

$$
D' = \begin{bmatrix}
- & 0.375 & 0.375 \\
0.375 & - & 0.30 \\
0.375 & 0.30 & -
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Next iteration joins Seq3 & Seq4 ($d_{34} = 0.30$), producing the tree:

```
((Seq1, Seq2), (Seq3, Seq4));
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper Style)]**

(a) *Explain the Feng-Doolittle progressive alignment algorithm. Discuss its advantages and limitations.* **[7 Marks]**

(b) *Compare ClustalW, MUSCLE, and MAFFT in terms of algorithm, accuracy, and computational complexity.* **[7 Marks]**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Feng-Doolittle Progressive Alignment

**Concept (Feng & Doolittle, 1987):**
A greedy heuristic for MSA based on three principles:
1. **Optimal global pairwise alignments** are computed for all sequence pairs.
2. A **guide tree** orders the alignment steps.
3. **Profile-profile alignment** merges the previously-aligned groups.

**Algorithm:**

```
Step 1: For every pair (i,j) of N sequences,
        compute a global alignment score S_ij.
Step 2: Convert S_ij to a distance d_ij.
Step 3: Construct a guide tree from the distance
        matrix using NJ or UPGMA.
Step 4: Perform progressive alignment by traversing
        the guide tree from leaves to root:
        - At each internal node, align the two child
          sub-alignments using profile-profile DP
          with affine gap penalties.
        - Store the merged alignment at the parent.
Step 5: The root alignment is the final MSA.
```

**Advantages:**
1. Reduces exponential DP ($O(L^N)$) to polynomial ($O(N L^2)$).
2. Conceptually simple and intuitive.
3. Leverages known pairwise alignments as building blocks.

**Limitations:**
1. **Greedy and Once-Aligned-Never-Revised**: an early misalignment propagates down the tree.
2. The **guide tree is approximate**; wrong tree topology produces suboptimal MSA.
3. Sensitive to **gap penalty parameter choice**.
4. No mechanism to handle **long terminal extensions** in distantly related sequences.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Comparison Table

| Feature | ClustalW | MUSCLE | MAFFT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year** | 1994 | 2004 | 2002 |
| **Algorithm** | Progressive + position-specific gaps | Progressive + iterative refinement | FFT-based + iterative |
| **Guide Tree** | NJ | Two trees (draft → refined) | NJ + fast k-mer |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(N^2 L^2) + O(N^3)$ | $O(N^3 L)$ to $O(N^4 L)$ | $O(N L \log L)$ for FFT mode |
| **Accuracy (BAliBASE)** | Moderate | High | High |
| **Memory** | Low | Moderate | Low–Moderate |
