<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define the Human Genome Project. State any two of its major objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
The Human Genome Project is an international collaborative research program launched in 1990 and completed in April 2003, aimed at mapping and sequencing the entire ~3.2 billion base pairs of the human genome and identifying all ~20,000–25,000 protein-coding genes.

Two major objectives:
1. To sequence the complete human genome with 99.99% accuracy and store the data in freely accessible public databases such as GenBank, EMBL and DDBJ.
2. To sequence the genomes of model organisms (*E. coli*, yeast, *Drosophila*, *C. elegans*, mouse) for comparative genomic benchmarking.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between Hierarchical Shotgun and Whole Genome Shotgun sequencing strategies.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hierarchical Shotgun | Whole Genome Shotgun |
|---|---|---|
| Map step | Requires pre-built BAC physical map | No physical map needed |
| Approach | Clone-by-clone | Random whole genome shearing |
| Speed | Slower | 3–5× faster |
| Repeats | Well resolved | Causes assembly collapse |
| Led by | Public HGP (Collins, Sulston) | Celera (Venter) |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions feature a choice. Both alternatives A and B are provided below. Answer **either** the full set.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the objectives, organizational structure, and the major milestones of the Human Genome Project. List the model organisms whose genomes were sequenced under the HGP. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Human Genome Project was a publicly funded, multinational initiative launched jointly by the **U.S. Department of Energy (DOE)** and the **National Institutes of Health (NIH)** in October 1990. It involved 18 nations including the USA, UK, Japan, France, Germany, China and India.

**Major Milestones:**
* 1990 — Official launch.
* 1995 — First free-living organism (*H. influenzae*) sequenced.
* 1996 — First eukaryotic genome (*S. cerevisiae*).
* 1999 — Chromosome 22 decoded (first human chromosome).
* June 26, 2000 — Working draft announced by Bill Clinton + Tony Blair.
* February 15, 2001 — Draft published in *Nature* (public) and *Science* (Celera).
* April 14, 2003 — "Finished" sequence announced, 2 years ahead of schedule.

**Model Organisms Sequenced:**
*Haemophilus influenzae*, *Mycoplasma genitalium*, *Escherichia coli*, *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, *Caenorhabditis elegans*, *Drosophila melanogaster*, *Arabidopsis thaliana*, *Mus musculus* (mouse), and *Rattus norvegicus* (rat).

**Objectives Recap:**
1. Identify all ~20,000–25,000 human genes.
2. Determine complete DNA sequence of 3.2 billion bp.
3. Store in GenBank/EMBL/DDBJ.
4. Develop bioinformatics tools (BLAST, BLAT).
5. Study ELSI implications.

**(b)** Explain the Hierarchical Shotgun (Clone-by-Clone) sequencing strategy in detail. How does it differ from the Whole Genome Shotgun approach used by Celera Genomics? Comment on coverage and quality achieved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Hierarchical Shotgun** strategy proceeds in a top-down fashion:

1. A **BAC library** with insert sizes of 100–200 kb is constructed from fragmented human DNA.
2. **STS (Sequence-Tagged Site) markers** are used to anchor BACs to chromosomal positions, producing a physical map.
3. A **minimal tiling path** of overlapping BACs is selected to span each chromosome.
4. Each BAC is sheared into 2–10 kb fragments and subcloned into plasmids.
5. These subclones are sequenced using the **Sanger dideoxy chain-termination method**.
6. Sequences are assembled into contigs, then integrated into the chromosome using the BAC map landmarks.

Celera's **Whole Genome Shotgun** strategy, in contrast, bypasses the physical map entirely:
1. The whole genome is sheared into 2 kb, 10 kb, and 50 kb fragments.
2. All fragments are sequenced in parallel.
3. Massive computing power applies **overlap-layout-consensus (OLC)** to assemble reads.

**Coverage & Quality Comparison:**
The hierarchical approach achieved **8–10× coverage** with 99.99% accuracy and minimal gaps. Celera's WGS achieved similar accuracy but was initially criticised for failing to resolve repetitive regions, particularly **segmental duplications** and **telomeric/centromeric repeats**. The two groups effectively merged their data in 2003 to produce the definitive human reference sequence.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Define sequence coverage. A sequencing project uses $2 \times 10^8$ reads of length 150 bp to sequence a genome of size $3 \times 10^9$ bp. Calculate the coverage depth. Comment on whether this is sufficient for a finished-grade assembly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Sequence coverage (or depth) $C$ is the average number of times each base of a genome is represented in the sequenced reads, given by:

$$
C = \dfrac{N \times L}{G}
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \dfrac{(2 \times 10^8) \times 150}{3 \times 10^9} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{3 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^9} \\[6pt]
&= 10 \times
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The genome is sequenced at $10\times$ coverage. According to the Lander–Waterman model, the probability that any base is **not** covered is:

$$
P(\text{uncovered}) = e^{-10} = 4.54 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.005\%
$$

Hence, ~99.995% of bases are covered, which is **sufficient for a finished-grade assembly** (the HGP itself used 8–10× as the standard for "finished" sequence with 99.99% accuracy).

**(b)** Discuss the ELSI issues that emerged from the Human Genome Project. Explain why gene patenting became a controversial issue. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The HGP allocated **3% to 5% of its annual budget** to a dedicated **ELSI (Ethical, Legal, Social Implications)** program, the largest bioethics effort ever embedded in a scientific project.

**Key ELSI Issues:**

1. **Privacy and Genetic Discrimination** — Whether insurers and employers may use genetic data to deny coverage or jobs. Led to the **Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (GINA, 2008)** in the USA.
2. **Fair Use of Genomic Data** — Who owns the genome? Should commercial entities patent individual genes?
3. **Informed Consent** — Especially for samples from indigenous populations (e.g., the Havasupai tribe case, 2010).
4. **Clinical Integration** — How to deliver personalised, gene-based medicine equitably.
5. **Psychological Impact** — Knowledge of predisposition to incurable diseases (e.g., Huntington's).
6. **Reproductive Choices** — Genetic screening of embryos and designer-baby concerns.

**Gene Patenting Controversy:**
Celera Genomics filed patents on ~6,500 gene fragments before their function was known. The **Association for Molecular Pathology v. Myriad Genetics (2013)** case eventually went to the U.S. Supreme Court, which ruled that **naturally occurring DNA segments are products of nature and cannot be patented**, though **cDNA (complementary DNA) is patentable** because it is synthetically created. This ruling opened the floodgates for affordable BRCA1/BRCA2 breast cancer testing, dropping from ~$4,000 to < $300.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Watch Out!):**
> 1. **Confusing BAC with YAC.** BACs use *E. coli* F-factor (100–300 kb), YACs use yeast (up to 2 Mb but unstable). Examiners deduct 1 mark if you swap them.
> 2. **Mis-stating coverage formula units.** Coverage is *dimensionless* (bp / bp), not a percentage. Saying "10% coverage" for 10× is a ½-mark error.
> 3. **Forgetting the "4-letter" and "23-chromosome" facts.** These are *expected to be quoted verbatim* in definitions.
> 4. **Mistaking the public draft date.** Draft announced **June 2000**, published **February 2001**, completed **April 2003** — these are three different events.
> 5. **Ignoring ELSI in 14-mark answers.** KTU's 2024 scheme maps to **CO5 (Ethics & Society)**; skipping ELSI forfeits up to 2 marks.
> 6. **Writing "100,000 genes"** — this was the *pre-HGP estimate*. The actual figure is 20,000–25,000. Examiners treat the higher number as outdated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorise for last-night exam prep):**

* ✅ HGP launched **1990**, completed **April 14, 2003**, 2 years ahead of schedule.
* ✅ Total **~3.2 × 10⁹ bp**; **~20,000–25,000** protein-coding genes; **24** chromosome types (22 + X + Y); 23 pairs.
* ✅ Two strategies: **Hierarchical Shotgun (public, Collins/Sulston, map-first)** vs **Whole Genome Shotgun (Celera, Venter, map-skip)**.
* ✅ **Coverage formula:** $C = (N \times L) / G$ — the *single most tested formula* in this module.
* ✅ **Lander–Waterman:** $P(\text{covered}) = 1 - e^{-C}$ — derive it for the 7-mark analytical sub-part.
* ✅ **Phred quality:** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$; $Q20 \Rightarrow 99\%$ accuracy, $Q30 \Rightarrow 99.9\%$.
* ✅ Finished sequence accuracy: **99.99%** (i.e., 1 error per 10,000 bp).
* ✅ Public HGP cost: **~$3 billion**; Celera: **~$300 million**; modern NGS: **<$200 per genome**.
* ✅ **Model organisms** sequenced: *H. influenzae, S. cerevisiae, C. elegans, D. melanogaster, M. musculus, A. thaliana*.
* ✅ **ELSI = Ethical, Legal, Social Implications** — 3–5% of budget; led to **GINA 2008**; gene patents struck down in **AMP v. Myriad 2013**.
* ✅ **First human chromosome decoded:** Chromosome **22** (1999).
* ✅ **First free-living organism sequenced:** *Haemophilus influenzae* (1995, TIGR, Fleischmann et al.).
* ✅ **cDNA is patentable; native genomic DNA is not** (Myriad ruling).
* ✅ **Reference assembly versions:** GRCh37 (2009) → **GRCh38** (2013) → T2T-CHM13 (2022, gap-free).
* ✅ Remember: SNPs, STS markers, BACs, YACs, cDNA libraries, dbSNP, GenBank are the **databases and tools** the HGP produced.

> 🎯 **Exam Tip:** When asked "Discuss the HGP", always cover **five pillars** — *(i) Goals, (ii) Methodology, (iii) Achievements, (iv) ELSI, (v) Post-genomic applications*. This 5-point structure is the KTU board examiner's gold standard for 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Next Generation Sequencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT615 - BIOINFORMATICS TECHNIQUES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Next Generation Sequencing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Next Generation Sequencing (NGS)**, also called **High-Throughput Sequencing (HTS)** or **Massively Parallel Sequencing (MPS)**, refers to a class of advanced DNA sequencing technologies that enable the simultaneous (parallel) sequencing of millions to billions of DNA fragments in a single instrument run, generating gigabases (Gb) to terabases (Tb) of nucleotide data within hours or days at a cost dramatically lower than the traditional Sanger method.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for **OEBBT615 – Bioinformatics Techniques and Applications**, Module 4 introduces NGS as a transformative technology that revolutionized genomics, transcriptomics, and personalized medicine by replacing the one-fragment-one-read paradigm with a highly multiplexed, miniaturized, parallelized workflow.

### 1.2 Evolution from Sanger to NGS

The traditional **Sanger Chain Termination Method** (developed by Frederick Sanger, 1977) could sequence only **~1,000 base pairs** per reaction, with a per-base cost of nearly **\$0.50–\$1.00** and requiring weeks of effort. The **Human Genome Project** (1990–2003), which relied on Sanger sequencing, cost approximately **\$2.7 billion** and took **13 years** to complete.

NGS platforms, emerging commercially after **2005**, can now sequence an entire human genome for under **\$200–\$600** in roughly **24 hours**, generating reads ranging from **50 bp** (Illumina) to over **>10,000 bp** (PacBio / Oxford Nanopore).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish **First Generation Sequencing** (Sanger), **Second Generation Sequencing** (Illumina, Ion Torrent, SOLiD), and **Third Generation Sequencing** (PacBio SMRT, Oxford Nanopore). This is a recurring 3-mark question.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Library Assembly Line

Imagine you have a **500-page novel (the genome)** and you want to read it. 

- **Sanger Sequencing (Old Way):** You hire **one reader** who reads the novel page-by-page from start to finish, taking weeks.
- **NGS (Modern Way):** You **photocopy** the novel into **millions of tiny 100-word snippets**, hand each snippet to a **different worker**, and have them **all read simultaneously**. Then you use a **computer (the assembler)** to piece the snippets together like a giant jigsaw puzzle by finding overlapping words.

This massive parallelism is the fundamental shift — instead of *one long read*, NGS produces *many short reads* that are computationally reassembled.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics in NGS

| Metric | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Read Length** | Number of base pairs in a single read | 50 bp – 25,000 bp |
| **Throughput** | Bases generated per run | 1 Gb – 6 Tb |
| **Coverage (Depth)** | Average number of times a base is sequenced | 30× – 100× (clinical: 30×) |
| **Accuracy (Q-score)** | Phred-scaled base-calling quality | Q20 – Q30 |
| **Run Time** | Time per instrument run | 2 hours – 11 days |

> [!NOTE]
> **Coverage Depth Formula:** A sequencing run producing **C** bases over a **G**-sized genome yields average depth  
> **D = C / G**. For example, sequencing a 3 Gb human genome at 30× depth requires **~90 Gb** of raw data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generalized NGS Workflow

Every NGS platform, regardless of manufacturer, follows a generalized five-stage workflow. KTU examiners frequently test the logical order of these steps.

**Stage 1 — Sample Preparation / DNA Extraction**
High-molecular-weight genomic DNA, cDNA (for RNA-seq), or amplicons are isolated from the biological source. Quality control (Qubit, NanoDrop) confirms purity (A260/A280 ≈ 1.8–2.0).

**Stage 2 — Library Preparation**
DNA is fragmented (enzymatically, acoustically, or by nebulization) into target sizes (e.g., 200–500 bp). Fragment ends are repaired, **adapters** are ligated, and fragments are PCR-amplified. For RNA-seq, an additional **cDNA synthesis** step occurs.

**Stage 3 — Cluster Generation / Template Amplification**
Sequencing templates are immobilized on a solid surface (flow cell or bead) and clonally amplified:
- **Bridge Amplification** (Illumina) → clusters on flow cell
- **Emulsion PCR (emPCR)** (Ion Torrent, 454) → beads in droplets
- **Single Molecule** (PacBio, Nanopore) → no amplification

**Stage 4 — Sequencing by Synthesis / Detection**
Nucleotide incorporation is detected optically (fluorescence) or electronically (pH change / current blockade).

**Stage 5 — Base Calling, Alignment & Variant Calling**
Raw signals (images or electrical traces) are converted to nucleotide sequences (FASTQ). Reads are aligned to a reference genome, and variants (SNPs, indels, SVs) are annotated.

### 2.2 Comparison of Major NGS Platforms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Comparison Table** — this exact comparison appears in multiple past model papers.

| Platform | Generation | Detection Method | Read Length | Throughput | Error Profile |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Illumina (MiSeq/NextSeq/NovaSeq)** | 2nd | Fluorescence (reversible terminator) | 50 – 600 bp | 1 Gb – 6 Tb / run | Low substitution errors |
| **Ion Torrent (PGM/S5)** | 2nd | pH change (H⁺ ion detection) | 200 – 600 bp | 0.5 – 25 Gb | Indels in homopolymers |
| **SOLiD** | 2nd | Two-base encoding fluorescence | 50 – 75 bp | 80 – 320 Gb | Lowest raw error rate |
| **PacBio (Sequel II)** | 3rd | Single Molecule Real-Time (SMRT) | 10 – 25 kb | 0.5 – 500 Gb | High (~10–15%), but high consensus accuracy |
| **Oxford Nanopore (MinION/PromethION)** | 3rd | Electrical current blockade | 1 kb – >2 Mb | 50 Gb – 14 Tb | Real-time; high indel error |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are **recurring in KTU ESE numericals and 3-mark derivations**.

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coverage Depth** | $D = \dfrac{C}{G}$ | $D$ = depth, $C$ = total sequenced bases, $G$ = genome size | Planning sequencing runs |
| **Phred Quality Score** | $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$ | $P_e$ = probability of base-calling error | Accuracy benchmarking |
| **Base-calling Error Probability** | $P_e = 10^{-Q/10}$ | $Q$ = phred score | Reverse calculation from Q |
| **Reads Required** | $R = \dfrac{D \times G}{L}$ | $R$ = number of reads, $D$ = depth, $G$ = genome size, $L$ = read length | Library design |
| **Lander-Waterman Genome Coverage** | $P_{cov} = 1 - e^{-D}$ | $D$ = depth, $P_{cov}$ = probability of coverage | Statistical coverage analysis |
| **GC Content** | $GC\% = \dfrac{G + C}{A + T + G + C} \times 100$ | A,T,G,C = nucleotide counts | Bias assessment |
| **Alignment Coverage** | $Cov_{align} = \dfrac{\text{Bases Aligned}}{\text{Reference Size}}$ | Coverage fraction | Variant calling QA |
| **Fold Change (RNA-seq)** | $FC = 2^{\Delta\Delta C_t}$ | $\Delta\Delta C_t$ = delta-delta threshold cycle | Differential expression |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Exam Trap:** Do **NOT** confuse *depth* with *breadth*. Depth = average reads per base; breadth = percentage of bases covered at least once.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Biomedical Utility

NGS is not just a "biology technique" — it is fundamentally an **engineering system** involving fluidics, optics, electronics, and bioinformatics pipelines.

- **Clinical Diagnostics:** Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT), cancer liquid biopsies (ctDNA), hereditary disease panels.
- **Agriculture:** Marker-assisted selection, livestock breeding (BRCA-analog screening in cattle).
- **Forensics & Biosecurity:** Pathogen surveillance (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 variant tracking).
- **Pharma:** Drug target identification, pharmacogenomics.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Metagenomic profiling of soil and wastewater microbiomes.
- **Synthetic Biology:** Whole-genome design verification in engineered organisms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Lander–Waterman Coverage Probability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Lander–Waterman Equation (1988)** is a foundational probability formula in NGS. KTU has asked derivations involving this in 14-mark problems.

**Statement:** Given a random sequencing library of read length $L$ and a haploid genome of size $G$, what is the probability $P_{cov}$ that a particular base is covered by at least one read?

**Step 1 — Define the problem in terms of Poisson approximation.**
Each base on the genome can be thought of as a "target." A single read is a random window of length $L$ on the genome. There are $R$ reads generated, each starting at one of $G$ possible positions (uniformly random).

The probability that a *specific* read covers a *specific* base is:
$$
p = \frac{L}{G}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the expected number of reads covering a base.**
Among $R$ independent reads, the expected count of reads covering base $i$ is:
$$
\mu = R \cdot p = \frac{R \cdot L}{G}
$$

But by the coverage formula $D = \dfrac{R \cdot L}{G}$, we have:
$$
\mu = D
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Poisson Approximation.**
The number of reads covering a given base $X$ follows approximately a **Poisson distribution** with mean $\mu = D$ (valid when $D$ is small relative to $L$).

Therefore:
$$
P(X = k) = \frac{D^{k} \cdot e^{-D}}{k!}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the probability of being uncovered.**
The probability that a base is covered by **zero** reads is $P(X=0)$:
$$
P(X = 0) = e^{-D}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute coverage probability $P_{cov}$.**
$$
P_{cov} = 1 - P(X = 0) = 1 - e^{-D}
$$

**Final Lander–Waterman Equation:**
$$
\boxed{P_{cov} = 1 - e^{-D}}
$$

**Step 6 — Worked Example.**
For the human genome ($G = 3 \times 10^{9}$ bp) sequenced at $D = 30\times$ depth:
$$
P_{cov} = 1 - e^{-30} \approx 1 - 9.36 \times 10^{-14} \approx 0.999999999999906
$$

Thus, **>99.9999%** of bases are covered at least once.

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Reads Required for Target Depth

**Problem:** A lab wishes to sequence *Escherichia coli* K-12 genome ($G = 4.6 \times 10^{6}$ bp) at $D = 50\times$ coverage using Illumina reads of length $L = 150$ bp. How many reads $R$ are required?

**Step 1 — Recall the relationship between reads, depth, read length, and genome size:**
$$
D = \frac{R \times L}{G}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R$:**
$$
R = \frac{D \times G}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute known values:**
$$
R = \frac{50 \times 4.6 \times 10^{6}}{150}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify numerator:**
$$
50 \times 4.6 \times 10^{6} = 230 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**
$$
R = \frac{230 \times 10^{6}}{150} = 1.5333 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{R \approx 1.53 \times 10^{6} \text{ reads}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Phred Score and Error Probability

**Step 1 — Phred Quality Definition.**
The Phred score $Q$ is defined such that an increase of 10 in $Q$ corresponds to a 10-fold reduction in error probability:
$$
Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P_e)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $P_e$:**
$$
\log_{10}(P_e) = -\frac{Q}{10}
$$

$$
P_e = 10^{-Q/10}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Examples.**

For $Q = 20$:
$$
P_e = 10^{-20/10} = 10^{-2} = 0.01 \;\; (99\% \text{ accuracy})
$$

For $Q = 30$:
$$
P_e = 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001 \;\; (99.9\% \text{ accuracy})
$$

For $Q = 40$:
$$
P_e = 10^{-40/10} = 10^{-4} = 0.0001 \;\; (99.99\% \text{ accuracy})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark:** Illumina's NovaSeq routinely delivers **>75% of bases at Q30 or higher**, which is the gold standard for clinical-grade sequencing.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing an NGS Platform

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests platform selection. This matrix is a high-yield visual aid.

| Application | Best Platform | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Whole Genome Sequencing (clinical)** | Illumina NovaSeq | Highest throughput, lowest per-base error |
| **SNP / small variant detection** | Illumina / BGI | High accuracy, short reads suffice |
| **Structural Variant / Large indel detection** | PacBio / Nanopore | Long reads span breakpoints |
| **RNA-seq quantification** | Illumina | High depth, stranded libraries |
| **Rapid pathogen ID (outbreak)** | Oxford Nanopore MinION | Portable, real-time basecalling |
| **Methylation profiling** | PacBio / Nanopore | Detects modified bases natively |
| **Low-input / single cell** | 10× Genomics + Illumina | Linked-read barcoding |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generalized NGS Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SAMPLE INPUT] --> B[DNA or RNA Extraction]
    B --> C[Quality Control QC]
    C --> D[Fragmentation]
    D --> E[End Repair and Adapter Ligation]
    E --> F[Size Selection]
    F --> G[Library Amplification PCR]
    G --> H[Cluster Generation on Flow Cell]
    H --> I[Sequencing by Synthesis or Detection]
    I --> J[Raw Signal Imaging or Electrical Trace]
    J --> K[Base Calling FASTQ Generation]
    K --> L[Quality Filtering Trimming]
    L --> M[Read Alignment to Reference]
    M --> N[Variant Calling or Assembly]
    N --> O[Downstream Annotation and Biological Interpretation]
```

### 4.2 Illumina Sequencing by Synthesis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Fragmented Library with Adapters] --> S2[Bind to Flow Cell Surface]
    S2 --> S3[Bridge Amplification - Cluster Formation]
    S3 --> S4[Linearization and Primer Hybridization]
    S4 --> S5[Incorporation of Fluorescently Labeled Reversible Terminator Nucleotides]
    S5 --> S6[Laser Excitation and Image Capture]
    S6 --> S7[Chemical Cleavage of Terminator and Fluorophore]
    S7 --> S8[Repeat Cycle N Times]
    S8 --> S9[Base Calling and FASTQ Output]
```

### 4.3 NGS Platform Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NGS[Next Generation Sequencing Technologies] --> SG1[Second Generation Sequencing]
    NGS --> SG2[Third Generation Sequencing]

    SG1 --> ILL[Illumina - Sequencing by Synthesis SBS]
    SG1 --> ION[Ion Torrent - Semiconductor Sequencing]
    SG1 --> SOL[SOLiD - Sequencing by Oligo Ligation Detection]

    SG2 --> PB[PacBio - Single Molecule Real Time SMRT]
    SG2 --> ONT[Oxford Nanopore - Nanopore Current Blockade]

    ILL --> ILLP[Miseq NextSeq NovaSeq]
    ION --> IONP[PGM S5 S5 XL]
    PB --> PBP[Sequel II Revio]
    ONT --> ONTP[MinION GridION PromethION]
```

### 4.4 NGS Data Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Raw FASTQ Files] --> P2[FastQC Quality Assessment]
    P2 --> P3[Adapter Trimming - Trimmomatic Cutadapt]
    P3 --> P4[Alignment to Reference - BWA Bowtie HISAT2]
    P4 --> P5[Post Alignment QC - Picard samtools]
    P5 --> P6[Variant Calling - GATK FreeBayes]
    P6 --> P7[Variant Filtering and Annotation - VEP ANNOVAR]
    P7 --> P8[Biological Interpretation and Clinical Reporting]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
**Define Next Generation Sequencing. List any two NGS platforms.**

**Model Answer:**
Next Generation Sequencing (NGS) refers to high-throughput, massively parallel DNA sequencing technologies that simultaneously sequence millions of DNA fragments, generating gigabases to terabases of data in a single instrument run. Examples: (i) Illumina (Sequencing by Synthesis) and (ii) Oxford Nanopore (nanopore-based electrical detection).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Differentiate between Illumina and Ion Torrent sequencing platforms.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Illumina | Ion Torrent |
|---|---|---|
| Detection | Fluorescence | pH change (H⁺ ions) |
| Read Length | 50–600 bp | 200–600 bp |
| Errors | Substitution | Indels in homopolymers |
| Amplification | Bridge PCR | emPCR on beads |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose 3–4 marks by (a) drawing the NGS workflow in the wrong sequence, (b) confusing depth vs breadth, (c) forgetting units in numerical answers, and (d) skipping the "real-world application" line at the end. Always **end with a conclusion sentence** for full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the generalized workflow of an Illumina NGS run, covering library preparation, cluster generation, sequencing by synthesis, and base calling.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Library Preparation (3 marks for this stage):**
- Genomic DNA is fragmented into 200–500 bp pieces (enzymatic or acoustic shearing).
- End repair converts sticky ends to blunt ends.
- 3′-dA overhang is added (A-tailing) to allow directional ligation.
- Platform-specific **adapters** (P5, P7, Index 1, Index 2) are ligated.
- Size selection via gel electrophoresis or bead-based cleanup.
- PCR amplification enriches properly ligated fragments.

**Step 2 — Cluster Generation (1 mark):**
- Library is loaded onto a **glass flow cell** coated with oligos complementary to the adapters.
- **Bridge amplification**: each fragment bends and hybridizes to a nearby oligo, creating a clonal cluster of ~1,000 copies per spot.
- This converts a single molecule into a detectable signal.

**Step 3 — Sequencing by Synthesis (2 marks):**
- Reversible terminator nucleotides (each labeled with a distinct fluorophore and blocked at the 3′-OH) are flowed across the cell.
- DNA polymerase incorporates one base per cycle.
- Lasers excite the fluorophore; a camera captures four images (one per channel).
- The terminator and fluorophore are chemically cleaved, and the next cycle begins.
- Cycles repeat for 50–600 cycles depending on the instrument.

**Step 4 — Base Calling and FASTQ Output (1 mark):**
- Image analysis identifies the incorporated base at each cluster.
- Quality scores (Phred Q) are assigned per base.
- Output is written in **FASTQ** format, containing read ID, sequence, and quality strings.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bacterial genome of size $4 \times 10^{6}$ bp is sequenced on an Illumina platform producing $5 \times 10^{6}$ reads of length 200 bp each. Calculate (i) total data generated, (ii) average depth of coverage, and (iii) the Lander–Waterman probability of a base being uncovered.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total data generated:**
$$
C = R \times L = 5 \times 10^{6} \times 200 = 1 \times 10^{9} \text{ bases} = 1 \text{ Gb}
$$

**(ii) Average depth of coverage:**
$$
D = \frac{C}{G} = \frac{1 \times 10^{9}}{4 \times 10^{6}} = 250 \times
$$

**(iii) Probability of a base being uncovered:**
Using Lander–Waterman:
$$
P_{uncovered} = e^{-D} = e^{-250} \approx 0
$$

Thus the genome is essentially completely covered.

**Conclusion:** A 250× depth is far higher than the typical 30–50× required, indicating over-sequencing; reducing reads to ~$10^{6}$ would suffice for $50\times$ coverage.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast Second Generation and Third Generation Sequencing technologies with suitable examples. Discuss their respective strengths and weaknesses.

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Second Generation (2GS) | Third Generation (3GS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Read Length** | Short (50–600 bp) | Long (10 kb – >2 Mb) |
| **Throughput** | Very high (up to 6 Tb/run) | Moderate to high (up to 14 Tb) |
| **Sample Prep** | Requires amplification | Single molecule, no amplification |
| **Error Rate** | Low (~0.1–0.5%) | Higher raw (~10–15%) but random; consensus accuracy >99.9% |
| **Time to Result** | Hours to days | Real-time (Nanopore) possible |
| **Cost per Mb** | Very low ($0.01–$0.10) | Higher ($0.10–$1.00) |
| **Examples** | Illumina, Ion Torrent, SOLiD | PacBio SMRT, Oxford Nanopore |
| **Strengths** | High accuracy, mature pipelines | Long reads span repeats, detect isoforms, native methylation |
| **Weaknesses** | Short reads struggle with repeats/isoforms | Higher raw error, higher cost, larger data |

**Conclusion:** 2GS dominates high-throughput clinical applications, while 3GS excels in de novo assembly, structural variant detection, and rapid field diagnostics.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the principles of Oxford Nanopore sequencing. A sequencing run produces **120,000 reads** of average length **8,000 bp** for a **3 Gb** human genome. Calculate the depth of coverage and the probability of a base being uncovered.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (5 marks):**
- A **nanopore** is a biological pore (e.g., *E. coli* CsgG or α-hemolysin) embedded in a synthetic membrane.
- A potential difference is applied across the membrane; an ionic current flows.
- As single-stranded DNA passes through the pore (driven by a motor enzyme), each k-mer of 4–6 bases produces a characteristic disruption in current.
- This **squiggle plot** is decoded in real time by a neural network (e.g., Guppy, Dorado) into nucleotide bases.
- Modified bases (e.g., 5-methylcytosine) are detected natively because they alter the current signal.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Total bases sequenced:
$$
C = 120{,}000 \times 8{,}000 = 9.6 \times 10^{8} \text{ bases}
$$

Average depth:
$$
D = \frac{C}{G} = \frac{9.6 \times 10^{8}}{3 \times 10^{9}} = 0.32 \times
$$

Probability of being uncovered:
$$
P_{uncovered} = e^{-D} = e^{-0.32} \approx 0.7261 \;\; (72.61\%)
$$

**Conclusion:** This sequencing effort is **grossly insufficient** for full human genome coverage. Approximately 28% of bases would be covered at least once. To achieve $D = 30\times$, the lab would need ~$11.25 \times 10^{6}$ reads.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **NGS = Massively Parallel Sequencing** generating Gb–Tb per run.
- ✅ **Three generations:** Sanger (1st) → Illumina/Ion Torrent/SOLiD (2nd) → PacBio/Nanopore (3rd).
- ✅ **Five-stage workflow:** Sample → Library → Cluster → Sequencing → Analysis.
- ✅ **Lander–Waterman equation:** $P_{cov} = 1 - e^{-D}$.
- ✅ **Coverage Depth:** $D = \dfrac{R \cdot L}{G}$.
- ✅ **Phred Score:** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$.
- ✅ **Illumina errors:** substitutions; **Ion Torrent errors:** indels in homopolymers; **Nanopore/PacBio errors:** random, corrected by consensus.
- ✅ **Bridge amplification** (Illumina) vs **emPCR** (Ion Torrent, 454) vs **Single molecule** (PacBio, Nanopore).
- ✅ **Clinical standard depth:** 30× for germline, 100×–1000× for tumor / liquid biopsy.
- ✅ **FASTQ** format: read ID + sequence + quality string.
- ✅ **Adapter contamination** must be trimmed before alignment.
- ✅ **Alignment tools:** BWA, Bowtie2, HISAT2; **Variant callers:** GATK, FreeBayes.
- ✅ **Long reads = better de novo assembly & SV detection; Short reads = cheaper, more accurate SNP calling.**
- ✅ **Q30 = 99.9% accuracy = industry benchmark for clinical reporting.**
- ✅ **Coverage Breadth ≠ Coverage Depth** (commonly confused in exams).
- ✅ **NGS revolutionized the Human Genome Project** — from \$2.7 B / 13 years to <\$600 / 24 hours.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always remember the **units**. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks when students forget to convert **Gb to bp** or vice-versa. $1 \text{ Gb} = 10^{9} \text{ bp}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection of SNPs and Their Relevance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT615 - BIOINFORMATICS TECHNIQUES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Detection of SNPs and Their Relevance

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of SNPs

A **Single Nucleotide Polymorphism (SNP)** is a stable, heritable variation at a single nucleotide position (A, T, G, or C) in the DNA sequence that is present in at least **1%** of a given population. SNPs represent the most abundant form of genetic variation in the human genome, occurring approximately once every **100–300 base pairs**.

$$ \text{SNP Frequency Threshold: } \geq 1\% \text{ of the population} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — SNP Definition Criteria**
> A variation qualifies as an **SNP** only if **all three** conditions are satisfied:
> 1. Variation occurs at a **single nucleotide** position (substitution, not indel).
> 2. The minor allele frequency (MAF) is **≥ 1%** in at least one population.
> 3. The variant is **germline-transmissible** (heritable through generations).

### 1.2 Classification of SNPs

SNPs are classified into several categories based on their location and mutational origin:

| Type | Definition | Example | Genomic Location |
|------|------------|---------|------------------|
| **Transition (Ts)** | Purine ↔ Purine or Pyrimidine ↔ Pyrimidine | A ↔ G, C ↔ T | ~2/3 of all SNPs |
| **Transversion (Tv)** | Purine ↔ Pyrimidine | A ↔ T, G ↔ C, etc. | ~1/3 of all SNPs |
| **Coding SNP (cSNP)** | Located within exons | rs334 (Sickle cell) | ~1% of SNPs |
| **Synonymous SNP** | No change in amino acid | — | Silent mutation |
| **Non-synonymous SNP** | Changes amino acid sequence | rs113488022 | Alters protein |
| **Intronic SNP** | Located within introns | — | Regulatory role |
| **Intergenic SNP** | Between genes | — | Often regulatory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Book Typo"**
> Imagine a 3-billion-letter instruction manual (your genome) that has been photocopied millions of times across humanity. An SNP is simply a **single typo** at one letter position in this manual — for example, where most people have a **"T"**, a small minority has an **"A"**. These tiny typos are individually trivial, but collectively, they form the molecular fingerprints that make each person unique, dictate drug response, and predispose individuals to diseases. SNP *detection* is therefore the art of finding these "typos" with extreme accuracy and scale.

### 1.3 The Central Dogma of SNP Relevance

SNPs serve three primary biological functions relevant to bioinformatics:
1. **Markers for linkage disequilibrium (LD)** in genome-wide association studies (GWAS).
2. **Causative variants** altering protein structure, splicing, or expression.
3. **Population-genetic signatures** for ancestry, evolution, and migration studies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Major SNP Detection Techniques

#### 2.1.1 Allele-Specific PCR (AS-PCR / ARMS)
- Uses **two forward primers** differing only at the 3′-terminal nucleotide (one matching each allele) paired with a common reverse primer.
- Only the perfectly matched primer is extended by Taq polymerase; mismatched primers fail to amplify.
- Detected via gel electrophoresis or **real-time PCR**.

#### 2.1.2 Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphism (RFLP)
- SNPs that alter a **restriction enzyme recognition site** produce different fragment sizes upon digestion.
- Visualization by agarose or polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis.

#### 2.1.3 Single-Strand Conformation Polymorphism (SSCP)
- Single-stranded DNA folds into a **sequence-specific 3D conformation**.
- Even a single base change alters migration through a non-denaturing polyacrylamide gel.
- Sensitivity: **>90%** for fragments <300 bp.

#### 2.1.4 High Resolution Melt (HRM) Analysis
- After PCR, amplicons are gradually heated.
- Sequence composition determines the **melting temperature (Tm)**.
- Intercalating fluorescent dye reports double-strand → single-strand transition.
- **Closed-tube, post-PCR detection** — no electrophoresis required.

#### 2.1.5 TaqMan Allelic Discrimination Assay
- Two allele-specific probes (VIC- and FAM-labeled) compete for binding during PCR.
- Endpoint fluorescence reports genotype: **homozygous (one signal), heterozygous (both signals)**.

#### 2.1.6 DNA Sequencing (Sanger & Next-Generation Sequencing)
- **Gold standard** for SNP detection.
- NGS enables **genome-wide SNP calling** at scale (millions of SNPs per sample).
- Algorithms: GATK HaplotypeCaller, FreeBayes, SAMtools/BCFtools.

#### 2.1.7 SNP Microarrays (DNA Chips)
- Pre-designed oligo probes immobilized on a solid surface.
- Hybridization of labeled sample DNA → fluorescent signal → genotype call.
- Used in **GWAS** (e.g., Illumina Infinium, Affymetrix Axiom).

#### 2.1.8 Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Time-of-Flight (MALDI-TOF MS)
- Primer extension products analyzed by mass.
- Each allele produces a distinct mass-to-charge ratio.

### 2.2 Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium — The Mathematical Backbone of SNP Analysis

For a biallelic SNP with alleles **A** (frequency *p*) and **a** (frequency *q*):

$$p + q = 1 \quad \text{(allele frequency constraint)}$$

The genotype frequencies at equilibrium are:

$$p^2 \text{ (AA)} : 2pq \text{ (Aa)} : q^2 \text{ (aa)}$$

And the **Hardy-Weinberg equation**:

$$p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of HWE**
> In bioinformatics pipelines, the **HWE test** (chi-square goodness-of-fit) is the **first QC step** for any genotype dataset. Departure from HWE indicates **genotyping error**, population stratification, or selection pressure at that locus.

### 2.3 Linkage Disequilibrium (LD) and the D′ Statistic

LD measures non-random association between alleles at two loci. The **D′ coefficient** ranges from 0 (no LD) to 1 (complete LD):

$$D = P_{AB} - P_A \cdot P_B$$

$$D' = \frac{D}{D_{\max}}$$

where $D_{\max} = \min(P_A P_b, p_a P_B)$ for the appropriate gametic configuration.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Allele Frequency | $p + q = 1$ | p, q = allele frequencies (dimensionless) | SNP genotyping |
| 2 | Hardy-Weinberg | $p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$ | genotype freq. | Population genetics |
| 3 | Heterozygosity | $H = 2pq$ | H = expected heterozygosity | Genetic diversity |
| 4 | Minor Allele Frequency | $\text{MAF} = \min(p, q)$ | MAF ∈ [0, 0.5] | SNP filtering |
| 5 | HWE Chi-square | $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}$ | df = 1 | QC of genotype data |
| 6 | Ts/Tv Ratio | $R = \frac{\text{Transitions}}{\text{Transversions}}$ | typical ≈ 2.0–3.0 | Sequencing QC |
| 7 | LOD Score | $\text{LOD} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L(\theta)}{L(0.5)}\right)$ | LOD ≥ 3 = significance | Linkage mapping |
| 8 | Genetic Distance | $\theta = 4 N_e c$ | $N_e$ = effective population size; c = recombination fraction | Population genetics |
| 9 | Linkage Disequilibrium | $D' = \frac{D}{D_{\max}}$ | $D' \in [0, 1]$ | Haplotype analysis |
| 10 | r² (LD Correlation) | $r^2 = \frac{D^2}{p_A p_a p_B p_b}$ | $r^2 \in [0, 1]$ | GWAS tag SNP selection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hardy-Weinberg Genotype Frequencies

We start from Mendelian random-mating assumptions. Let **A** and **a** be the two alleles at an autosomal SNP locus with frequencies *p* and *q*, where $p + q = 1$.

**Step 1 — Setup the random-mating probability.**
In a randomly mating diploid population, the allele inherited from the father is independent of the allele inherited from the mother. Hence, the probability of any genotype is the **product** of the parental allele probabilities.

**Step 2 — Enumerate all possible genotype combinations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(AA) &= p \cdot p = p^2 \\[4pt]
P(Aa) &= p \cdot q + q \cdot p = 2pq \\[4pt]
P(aa) &= q \cdot q = q^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the law of total probability.**
The sum of all genotype probabilities must equal **1**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(AA) + P(Aa) + P(aa) &= 1 \\[4pt]
p^2 + 2pq + q^2 &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic identity verification.**
Recognize the expression as the **square of a binomial**:

$$
(p + q)^2 = p^2 + 2pq + q^2
$$

Since $p + q = 1$, the right-hand side simplifies to $1^2 = 1$. ✓ This proves HWE consistency.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Expected Heterozygosity (H)

The expected heterozygosity is the probability that a randomly chosen individual carries **two different alleles**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 2pq
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting** $q = 1 - p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 2p(1 - p) = 2p - 2p^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Finding the maximum** of H:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dH}{dp} &= 2 - 4p = 0 \\[4pt]
p &= 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, **maximum heterozygosity** $H_{\max} = 2(0.5)(0.5) = 0.5$, occurring when both alleles have equal frequency. This means a biallelic SNP can never exceed **50% heterozygosity** — a fundamental result that distinguishes SNPs from microsatellites.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — HWE Chi-Square Test for a SNP

Suppose in a cohort of **n = 200** individuals, an SNP is genotyped as follows:

| Genotype | Observed (O) |
|----------|-------------|
| AA | 90 |
| Aa | 90 |
| aa | 20 |
| **Total** | **200** |

**Step 1 — Compute allele frequencies.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Count of A} &= 2(90) + 90 = 270 \\[4pt]
\text{Count of a} &= 2(20) + 90 = 130 \\[4pt]
\text{Total alleles} &= 2 \times 200 = 400 \\[4pt]
p &= \frac{270}{400} = 0.675 \\[4pt]
q &= \frac{130}{400} = 0.325
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute expected genotype counts under HWE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(AA) &= p^2 \cdot n = (0.675)^2 \cdot 200 = 0.4556 \cdot 200 \approx 91.1 \\[4pt]
E(Aa) &= 2pq \cdot n = 2(0.675)(0.325) \cdot 200 = 0.4388 \cdot 200 \approx 87.8 \\[4pt]
E(aa) &= q^2 \cdot n = (0.325)^2 \cdot 200 = 0.1056 \cdot 200 \approx 21.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the chi-square statistic.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(90 - 91.1)^2}{91.1} + \frac{(90 - 87.8)^2}{87.8} + \frac{(20 - 21.1)^2}{21.1} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.21}{91.1} + \frac{4.84}{87.8} + \frac{1.21}{21.1} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0133 + 0.0551 + 0.0573 \\[4pt]
\chi^2 &\approx 0.126
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule.**

For **df = 1** (one degree of freedom after estimating *p* from the data), the critical value at α = 0.05 is **3.84**.

Since $\chi^2 = 0.126 < 3.84$, we **fail to reject HWE** → this SNP is in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. ✓

---

### 3.4 Derivation of LOD Score Threshold for Linkage

The **LOD score** (logarithm of the odds) for linkage between an SNP marker and a disease locus is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LOD} &= \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L(\theta)}{L(0.5)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $L(\theta)$ is the likelihood of observing the pedigree data given recombination fraction $\theta$, and $L(0.5)$ is the likelihood under the null hypothesis of **no linkage**.

**Conventional thresholds:**

| LOD Score | Interpretation |
|-----------|----------------|
| LOD < −2 | Exclusion of linkage |
| −2 ≤ LOD < 3 | Inconclusive |
| **LOD ≥ 3** | **Significant linkage** (odds 1000:1 in favor) |
| LOD ≥ 1 | Suggestive linkage |

**Proof of significance threshold:**
LOD = 3 corresponds to odds ratio of $10^3 : 1 = 1000 : 1$, the conventional genome-wide significance threshold proposed by Morton (1955).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SNP Detection Workflow — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Genomic DNA Sample] --> B[DNA Extraction & QC]
    B --> C[DNA Quantification]
    C --> D{Select SNP Detection Platform}
    D --> E1[AS-PCR / ARMS]
    D --> E2[RFLP]
    D --> E3[SSCP / DGGE]
    D --> E4[HRM Analysis]
    D --> E5[TaqMan qPCR]
    D --> E6[Sequencing Sanger NGS]
    D --> E7[SNP Microarray]
    D --> E8[MALDI TOF MS]
    E1 --> F[Raw Signal Acquisition]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    E7 --> F
    E8 --> F
    F --> G[Bioinformatics Pipeline]
    G --> H1[Quality Control FASTQ]
    G --> H2[Alignment BWA Bowtie]
    G --> H3[Variant Calling GATK FreeBayes]
    G --> H4[Annotation VEP ANNOVAR]
    H1 --> I[HWE Filtering]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Functional & Clinical Interpretation]
    J --> K1[Disease Association GWAS]
    J --> K2[Pharmacogenomics]
    J --> K3[Population Genetics]
    J --> K4[Personalized Medicine]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Decision Matrix for SNP Detection Methods

```mermaid

    A[SNP Detection Choice] --> B{Low Throughput}
    A --> C{Medium Throughput}
    A --> D{High Throughput}

```

### 4.3 SNP Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid

    SNP[SNP] --> L1[Location Based]
    SNP --> L2[Functional Effect]
    L1 --> C1[Coding cSNP]
    L1 --> C2[Non Coding ncSNP]

    L2 --> F2[Non Synonymous Missense]
    L2 --> F3[Nonsense Stop Gain]
    L2 --> F4[Splice Site]

    F4 --> P
```
