
> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Board Exams**
> When asked to draw an SNP detection workflow in the answer script, always label each box with both **technique name** and **detection principle** (e.g., "AS-PCR — 3′-mismatch discrimination"). Examiners award marks for both **process flow** and **technical clarity**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define Single Nucleotide Polymorphism. Distinguish between transition and transversion with examples.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Single Nucleotide Polymorphism (SNP)** is a stable substitution of one nucleotide by another at a specific position in the genome that is present in **≥ 1%** of the population.

- **Transition:** Purine ↔ Purine (A ↔ G) or Pyrimidine ↔ Pyrimidine (C ↔ T). Caused by similarity in chemical structure.
- **Transversion:** Purine ↔ Pyrimidine (e.g., A ↔ T, G ↔ C). Structurally more disruptive.

Transitions are **2–3× more frequent** than transversions due to the chemical similarity of purines and pyrimidines within their own class.

---

**Q2. What is Minor Allele Frequency (MAF)? How is it computed from genotype data?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
**MAF** is the frequency of the **less common** allele at a polymorphic locus in a given population.

If $n_A$ = count of major allele and $n_a$ = count of minor allele:

$$p = \frac{n_A}{n_A + n_a}, \quad q = \frac{n_a}{n_A + n_a}$$

$$\text{MAF} = \min(p, q)$$

**Significance:** SNPs with MAF < 0.01 are typically **excluded** from GWAS because they lack statistical power. MAF filtering is a **standard QC step** in pipelines like PLINK.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Students answer **one** of two questions from the same module, carrying 7 marks per sub-part.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) Explain in detail the principle, procedure, advantages, and limitations of any three SNP detection methods.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Allele-Specific PCR (AS-PCR / ARMS)**
- **Principle:** Taq polymerase extends only primers with a perfect 3′-match; mismatched primers fail.
- **Procedure:** Two forward primers (one per allele) differing at the 3′-end + common reverse primer → separate PCR reactions or multiplex with different fluorophores.
- **Advantages:** Low cost, rapid, no specialized equipment beyond a thermocycler.
- **Limitations:** Sensitive to reaction conditions; optimization needed to suppress false positives.

**2. Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphism (RFLP)**
- **Principle:** SNP creates or abolishes a restriction enzyme site → altered fragment length after digestion.
- **Procedure:** PCR amplify region → digest with specific enzyme → electrophorese.
- **Advantages:** Robust, inexpensive, no labeling required.
- **Limitations:** Only detects SNPs that **disrupt** restriction sites; not all SNPs are RFLP-able.

**3. Single-Strand Conformation Polymorphism (SSCP)**
- **Principle:** Single-stranded DNA adopts sequence-dependent 3D folds; mutations alter electrophoretic mobility.
- **Procedure:** Denature dsDNA → load on non-denaturing PAGE → silver stain or autoradiography.
- **Advantages:** Can detect SNPs in **unknown positions** within a fragment.
- **Limitations:** Optimization of temperature and gel conditions is critical; not suitable for high throughput.

---

**(b) Describe the applications of SNPs in pharmacogenomics and personalized medicine with suitable examples.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Pharmacogenomics** studies how genetic variation influences drug response. SNPs in genes encoding **drug-metabolizing enzymes, transporters, and targets** dictate therapeutic efficacy and adverse reactions.

**Key Examples:**

| Gene | SNP (rsID) | Drug Affected | Clinical Outcome |
|------|------------|---------------|------------------|
| **CYP2C19** | rs4244285 (*2) | Clopidogrel | Poor metabolizer → reduced activation → stent thrombosis risk |
| **TPMT** | rs1800462 | 6-Mercaptopurine | Deficient activity → severe myelosuppression |
| **VKORC1** | rs9923231 | Warfarin | Increased sensitivity → lower dose required |
| **CYP2D6** | rs1065852 | Codeine | Ultrarapid metabolizer → morphine toxicity |
| **HLA-B** | rs2395029 | Abacavir | Hypersensitivity syndrome in carriers |

**Personalized Medicine:** SNP profiling enables **pre-emptive dose adjustment**, **drug selection**, and **risk stratification**, transforming "one-size-fits-all" prescriptions into genotype-guided therapy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the workflow of SNP detection by Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS). Explain the role of alignment and variant calling tools.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**NGS Workflow:**

1. **Library Preparation:** Fragment genomic DNA → ligate adapters → PCR amplification.
2. **Sequencing:** Cluster generation on flow cell → sequencing-by-synthesis (Illumina) or other chemistries → raw reads (FASTQ).
3. **Quality Control:** FASTQC checks per-base quality, GC content, adapter contamination.
4. **Alignment:** Reads mapped to reference genome using **BWA-MEM** or **Bowtie2** → output as **SAM/BAM** file.
5. **Post-alignment Processing:** Sorting, marking duplicates (**Picard**), base quality recalibration (**GATK BQSR**).
6. **Variant Calling:** Identify SNPs/indels using:
   - **GATK HaplotypeCaller** — local de novo assembly around variants.
   - **FreeBayes** — Bayesian haplotype-based caller.
   - **SAMtools/BCFtools** — pileup-based calling.
7. **Variant Filtering:** Apply quality thresholds (QUAL, QD, FS, MQ), HWE, MAF.
8. **Annotation:** Use **VEP** or **ANNOVAR** to predict functional impact.

**Role of Tools:**
- **Alignment:** Determines position of each read; mismatches vs. reference are candidate variants.
- **Variant Callers:** Statistically distinguish true variants from sequencing errors using base quality, mapping quality, and read depth.

---

**(b) Discuss the role of SNPs in Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS). Explain the concept of Linkage Disequilibrium (LD) and tag SNPs.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**GWAS — Concept:** A GWAS scans hundreds of thousands to millions of SNPs across the genome to identify variants statistically associated with a trait or disease. Significance threshold is typically $p < 5 \times 10^{-8}$ (Bonferroni-corrected).

**Workflow:**
1. Genotype large case-control cohort using SNP arrays.
2. Apply QC filters (MAF > 0.01, HWE $p > 10^{-6}$, call rate > 98%).
3. Statistical association test (chi-square or logistic regression) per SNP.
4. Generate **Manhattan plot** displaying $-\log_{10}(p)$ vs. chromosomal position.

**Linkage Disequequilibrium (LD):** Non-random association between alleles at nearby loci. Measured by **D′** and **r²**. LD decays with **recombination** and **physical distance**.

**Tag SNPs:** In regions of high LD, a few SNPs capture the genetic variation of many. GWAS arrays are designed around **tag SNPs** — genotyping ~500,000 SNPs can tag >10 million common variants in Europeans due to LD structure.

**Significance:** LD-based tag SNP strategy makes GWAS **cost-effective**, but limits **fine-mapping resolution** — identifying the causal variant requires additional sequencing in the associated LD block.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "SNP" with "mutation":** Mutations can be any frequency; SNPs **must** have MAF ≥ 1%.
> 2. **Forgetting the HWE assumption:** When deriving genotype frequencies, you MUST explicitly state "random mating, no selection, no migration, infinite population."
> 3. **Skipping the chi-square step in HWE problems:** Examiners award **2 marks** for the explicit χ² computation, not just the formula.
> 4. **Naming tools without describing their role:** Writing "GATK" alone is insufficient — describe HaplotypeCaller's **Bayesian model** or **local de novo assembly** to earn full marks.
> 5. **Missing "tag SNP" in GWAS answers:** This is a **guaranteed 2-mark question** in recent KTU papers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SNP Definition:** Single nucleotide substitution present in ≥ 1% of the population.
- **Two major classes:** Transitions (purine↔purine, pyrimidine↔pyrimidine) and Transversions (purine↔pyrimidine).
- **MAF** filters rare variants; **HWE** filters genotyping errors.
- **Hardy-Weinberg Equation:** $p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$ — foundation of population genetics.
- **Detection Methods Spectrum:** Low-throughput (AS-PCR, RFLP, SSCP) → Mid-throughput (HRM, TaqMan) → High-throughput (NGS, SNP arrays, MALDI-TOF).
- **NGS is the gold standard** but most expensive; SNP arrays dominate GWAS due to cost-efficiency via tag SNPs.
- **Pharmacogenomic Relevance:** SNPs in **CYP450 genes** dictate drug metabolism — critical for personalized medicine.
- **GWAS Significance Threshold:** $p < 5 \times 10^{-8}$ (Bonferroni-corrected for ~1 million tests).
- **LOD ≥ 3** = significant linkage in family-based studies.
- **Ts/Tv Ratio:** Expected ~2–3 in true SNPs; lower values suggest false positives.
- **LD Metrics:** D′ (historical recombination), r² (correlation — preferred for tag SNP selection).
- **rsID Convention:** All SNPs catalogued in **dbSNP** (NCBI) carry unique identifiers prefixed with **"rs"**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection of Functional Sites in DNA Sequences
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT615 - BIOINFORMATICS TECHNIQUES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Detection of Functional Sites in DNA Sequences

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In computational biology and bioinformatics, **functional sites** in DNA sequences refer to specific short nucleotide subsequences (typically **6–50 base pairs** in length) that carry out a defined biochemical, regulatory, or structural role in the cell. They act as biologically meaningful *signals* embedded in the largely non-coding genomic text. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Bioinformatics Techniques and Applications*, the detection of these sites is one of the foundational problems in genome annotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024):**
> A *functional site* is a short, conserved DNA motif whose presence, position, and sequence composition determine a specific molecular event — such as transcription initiation, translation initiation, RNA splicing, transcription factor binding, polyadenylation, or replication origin firing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Signage" View

Think of a long DNA molecule (which in humans stretches over **3 billion characters**) as a **national highway running across a country**. The asphalt itself is largely the same, but along the route there are **signals, signals, signals**:

- **Green/Red lights** = Promoters that say "start transcribing here"
- **Lane-change arrows** = Transcription factor binding sites that say "express this gene only in liver cells"
- **Exit ramps** = Splice sites that say "remove this intron"
- **Stop signs** = Stop codons that say "end translation"
- **Service stations** = Translation initiation sites that say "begin protein synthesis"
- **Postal codes** = Polyadenylation signals that say "add the mRNA tail"

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a single missing or damaged sign causes accidents, a single mutation at a functional site (e.g., a promoter mutation) can cause a disease such as **β-thalassemia** or cancer. Detection of these signs computationally is therefore a critical first step in understanding gene function and disease.

### 1.3 Major Categories of Functional Sites (High-Yield for KTU)

| Category | Example Functional Site | Typical Length | Biological Role |
|----------|------------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Promoter elements | **TATA box**, **CAAT box**, **GC box** | 6–10 bp | Recruit RNA polymerase II |
| Transcription factor binding sites (TFBS) | SP1, AP-1, NF-κB motifs | 6–20 bp | Regulate gene expression |
| Translation initiation site | **Kozak sequence** (gccRccAUGG) | ~10 bp | Ribosome assembly start |
| Splice sites | **GT–AG rule** (donor/acceptor) | 2 bp (canonical) | Intron removal |
| Stop codon | TAA, TAG, TGA | 3 bp | Translation termination |
| Polyadenylation signal | **AAUAAA** | 6 nt | mRNA 3′-end formation |
| CpG islands | CG-rich regions | ≥200 bp, GC ≥ 50% | Often mark promoters |
| Ribosome binding site (prokaryotes) | **Shine–Dalgarno** (AGGAGG) | 6 nt | 16S rRNA pairing |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 module specifically emphasizes **promoter prediction, TFBS detection, splice site identification, and the use of Position Weight Matrices (PWMs) and Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)** as the canonical computational tools for these tasks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Detection Problem — A Formal Statement

Given a DNA sequence $S = s_1 s_2 s_3 \ldots s_L$ of length $L$, the goal is to determine:

1. **Does** a functional site of type $T$ occur in $S$?
2. **Where** does it occur (position $i$)?
3. **How confident** are we in the prediction (a $p$-value or score)?

This is formulated as a **statistical pattern recognition** problem where one discriminates *site* sequences from *non-site* (background) sequences.

### 2.2 Approach 1 — Consensus Sequence (Naïve Method)

If we align $N$ known examples of a site, the **consensus** is the most frequent base at each position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** Discards quantitative information about *how strongly* each base is preferred. Two bases occurring 49% and 51% of the time are treated identically, even though the latter is far more informative.

### 2.3 Approach 2 — Position Weight Matrix (PWM) / Position-Specific Scoring Matrix (PSSM)

A PWM assigns a numerical weight to each nucleotide at each position of the site. It is the **workhorse method** in TFBS detection and is a KTU high-yield topic.

**Construction Steps (Why & How):**

1. **Collect training set** of $N$ aligned functional sites of length $W$.
2. **Count** the occurrence $n_{i,b}$ of base $b \in \{A, C, G, T\}$ at position $i$.
3. **Compute observed frequency** $f_{i,b} = n_{i,b} / N$.
4. **Pseudocount correction** (Laplace rule) avoids zero entries:
   $f_{i,b}^{'} = \dfrac{n_{i,b} + p_b \cdot \alpha}{N + \alpha}$, where $\alpha$ is the pseudocount total and $p_b$ the background prior.
5. **Convert to log-odds score** against a background model (typically uniform or genomic $G+C$ content).

### 2.4 Approach 3 — Hidden Markov Model (HMM) Detection

For sites of variable length or with internal structure (e.g., eukaryotic promoters with TATA + Inr), an **HMM** is superior. The HMM has:
- A *background* state (emits random bases),
- A *site* state or set of states (emits motif-consistent bases),
- **Emission probabilities** and **transition probabilities** learned from data.

Detection is then performed via the **Viterbi algorithm**, which finds the most probable state path producing the observed sequence.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these six equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU Part A and Part B questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Pseudocount-corrected frequency | $f_{i,b}^{'} = \dfrac{n_{i,b} + p_b \, \alpha}{N + \alpha}$ | Laplace/Bayesian smoothing |
| 2 | PWM log-odds score | $S_{i,b} = \log_2 \left( \dfrac{f_{i,b}^{'}}{p_b} \right)$ | $p_b$ = background freq of base $b$ |
| 3 | Total PWM score of a window | $S_{window} = \sum_{i=1}^{W} S_{i, s_i}$ | Sum of per-position log-odds |
| 4 | Information content of a site (per position) | $R_i = 2 - H_i$, where $H_i = -\sum_b f_{i,b} \log_2 f_{i,b}$ | $R_i$ in bits; max = 2 bits |
| 5 | Total information content of motif | $R_{seq} = \sum_{i=1}^{W} R_i$ | Sequence logo height |
| 6 | Viterbi recursion | $V_{k}(j) = \max_{i} \left[ V_{i}(j-1) \cdot a_{ik} \right] \cdot e_k(s_j)$ | Used in HMM site detection |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, vertical bars are deliberately avoided; absolute value is written using the `\\vert` command in actual LaTeX rendering to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.6 Engineering / Real-World Utility

- **Personalized medicine:** Detecting promoter or splice-site mutations in patient DNA for **cystic fibrosis**, **spinal muscular atrophy**, and many cancers.
- **Synthetic biology:** Designing promoters with desired strengths in engineered yeast/bacteria.
- **Drug discovery:** Identifying TFBS for targets (e.g., **p53** in cancer therapy).
- **Forensics & agriculture:** Detecting functional sites in pathogen genomes and in crop-improvement programs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation — Building a PWM from Scratch

**Problem (KTU-style):**  
Suppose we have aligned **6 binding sites** of a hypothetical transcription factor of length $W = 4$:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \text{ACGT} \\
S_2 &= \text{ACGT} \\
S_3 &= \text{ACAT} \\
S_4 &= \text{TCGT} \\
S_5 &= \text{ACGT} \\
S_6 &= \text{GCGT}
\end{aligned}$$

The genomic background frequencies are $p_A = 0.3$, $p_C = 0.2$, $p_G = 0.2$, $p_T = 0.3$. Use pseudocount $\alpha = 1$ (with equal prior $p_b^{prior} = 0.25$).

**Step 1 — Build the count matrix** by counting base occurrences at each position $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$:

| Position | A | C | G | T |
|----------|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 6 | 0 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 6 |

**Step 2 — Apply pseudocount correction** using

$$f_{i,b}^{'} = \frac{n_{i,b} + p_b^{prior} \cdot \alpha}{N + \alpha} = \frac{n_{i,b} + 0.25}{6 + 1} = \frac{n_{i,b} + 0.25}{7}$$

For position 1:
- $f_{1,A}^{'} = (4 + 0.25)/7 = 4.25/7 = 0.6071$
- $f_{1,C}^{'} = (0 + 0.25)/7 = 0.0357$
- $f_{1,G}^{'} = (1 + 0.25)/7 = 0.1786$
- $f_{1,T}^{'} = (1 + 0.25)/7 = 0.1786$

For position 2 (only C observed):
- $f_{2,C}^{'} = (6 + 0.25)/7 = 0.8929$, others each $= 0.0357$.

For positions 3 and 4 the calculations are analogous (G-only and T-only, respectively).

**Step 3 — Convert to log-odds scores** using

$$S_{i,b} = \log_2 \left( \frac{f_{i,b}^{'}}{p_b} \right)$$

For position 1:
$$\begin{aligned}
S_{1,A} &= \log_2(0.6071 / 0.3) = \log_2(2.024) = +1.017 \text{ bits} \\
S_{1,C} &= \log_2(0.0357 / 0.2) = \log_2(0.179) = -2.485 \text{ bits} \\
S_{1,G} &= \log_2(0.1786 / 0.2) = \log_2(0.893) = -0.163 \text{ bits} \\
S_{1,T} &= \log_2(0.1786 / 0.3) = \log_2(0.595) = -0.748 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Positive scores indicate the base is *more frequent than background*; negative scores indicate *less frequent than background*. The strongest preferred base at position 1 is **A** with $+1.017$ bits.

For position 2 (C is dominant):
$$\begin{aligned}
S_{2,C} &= \log_2(0.8929 / 0.2) = \log_2(4.464) = +2.159 \text{ bits} \\
S_{2,A} &= \log_2(0.0357 / 0.3) = -3.072 \text{ bits} \\
S_{2,G} &= \log_2(0.0357 / 0.2) = -2.485 \text{ bits} \\
S_{2,T} &= \log_2(0.0357 / 0.3) = -3.072 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Score an arbitrary query window**, say $Q = \text{ACGT}$:

$$S_{ACGT} = S_{1,A} + S_{2,C} + S_{3,G} + S_{4,T}$$

Substituting the values (with $S_{3,G} = +2.159$ bits and $S_{4,T} = +2.159$ bits by analogous derivation):

$$S_{ACGT} = (+1.017) + (+2.159) + (+2.159) + (+2.159) = +7.494 \text{ bits}$$

This is a **strong positive score** — a sliding-window search across a genome would flag this window as a likely binding site.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Information Content of a Site

For a perfectly conserved position (e.g., position 2 with $f_{2,C} = 0.8929$):

$$H_2 = -\sum_{b} f_{2,b} \log_2 f_{2,b} = -[0.0357 \log_2 0.0357 + 0.8929 \log_2 0.8929 + 0.0357 \log_2 0.0357 + 0.0357 \log_2 0.0357]$$

$$H_2 = -[3 \times 0.0357 \times (-4.808) + 0.8929 \times (-0.163)] = 0.515 + 0.146 = 0.661 \text{ bits}$$

$$R_2 = 2 - H_2 = 2 - 0.661 = 1.339 \text{ bits}$$

For a fully conserved position (entropy $= 0$): $R_i = 2$ bits — the maximum. This explains the height of letters in a **sequence logo** (e.g., the **WebLogo** tool).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison

| Method | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best Use Case |
|--------|-----------|------------|---------------|
| Consensus | Simple, intuitive | Loses quantitative info | Quick scanning, teaching |
| PWM / PSSM | Quantitative, easy to combine | Assumes independence between positions | TFBS, short fixed-length motifs |
| HMM | Handles variable length, dependencies | Needs many training examples, parameter training | Gene finders, splice sites, promoters |
| Neural Network (NN) | Learns complex patterns | Black box, needs large training set | Splice site prediction (e.g., NetGene2) |
| Comparative genomics | Evolutionary grounded | Requires multiple aligned genomes | Regulatory element detection |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Site Detection — Master Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input DNA Sequence S of length L] --> B[Sliding Window of Width W]
    B --> C[Extract Candidate Window w]
    C --> D[Score using PWM or HMM]
    D --> E{Score above threshold T}
    E -- Yes --> F[Flag as Predicted Functional Site]
    E -- No --> G[Discard]
    F --> H[Output Annotated Sequence with Site Coordinates]
    G --> B
```

### 4.2 PWM Construction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Training Set of N Aligned Sites] --> B[Count Matrix n i b]
    B --> C[Pseudocount Correction]
    C --> D[Frequency Matrix f prime i b]
    D --> E[Divide by Background p b]
    E --> F[Log base 2]
    F --> G[Log Odds PWM Matrix]
    G --> H[Sliding Window Scoring of Genome]
    H --> I[Threshold Filter]
    I --> J[Final Predictions]
```

### 4.3 HMM Architecture for Splice Site Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BG[Background State] -- a1 --> EX[Exon State]
    EX -- a2 --> D5[Donor Splice Site GT]
    D5 -- a3 --> IN[Intron State]
    IN -- a4 --> A3[Acceptor Splice Site AG]
    A3 -- a5 --> EX2[Next Exon State]
    BG -.b1.-> BG
    EX -.b2.-> EX
```

### 4.4 Sequence Logo — Conceptual Render

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Position 1: A 2.0 bits] --> P2[Position 2: C 1.5 bits]
    P2 --> P3[Position 3: G 1.8 bits]
    P3 --> P4[Position 4: T 2.0 bits]
    P4 --> P5[Position 5: A 1.2 bits]
    P5 --> P6[Position 6: A 1.9 bits]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each "block" in the conceptual logo above represents the **information content** $R_i$ at that position. Taller blocks indicate stronger evolutionary conservation at that position of the motif.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[Q1. KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]**  
> **Define a Position Weight Matrix (PWM). How is it constructed from a set of aligned functional sites? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A **Position Weight Matrix (PWM)**, also called a **Position-Specific Scoring Matrix (PSSM)**, is a $4 \times W$ numerical matrix that quantifies the preference of each nucleotide at each position of a functional site of length $W$. Construction proceeds in three steps:
1. Collect $N$ aligned known sites and build a $4 \times W$ count matrix $n_{i,b}$.
2. Normalize with pseudocounts to obtain $f_{i,b}^{'} = (n_{i,b} + p_b \alpha) / (N + \alpha)$.
3. Convert to log-odds scores: $S_{i,b} = \log_2(f_{i,b}^{'} / p_b)$.  
A candidate sequence window is scored by summing per-position log-odds, and windows above a threshold are predicted as sites. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[Q2. KTU University Exam — June 2024, Retrospective Pattern]**  
> **What is the information content of a fully conserved position in a DNA motif? Justify with the formula. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The information content of a position is defined as

$$R_i = 2 - H_i, \quad \text{where } H_i = -\sum_{b \in \{A,C,G,T\}} f_{i,b} \log_2 f_{i,b}$$

For a **fully conserved** position, one base has frequency 1 and the others have frequency 0 (limit), giving $H_i = 0$. Therefore

$$R_i^{\max} = 2 - 0 = 2 \text{ bits}$$

The value of **2 bits** corresponds to the maximum information content per DNA position (since $\log_2 4 = 2$). This is the height of the tallest letter in a **WebLogo** sequence logo. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Q3. KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Q-style]**  
> **(a)** Explain the biological significance and structure of the **TATA box** and the **Kozak sequence** as functional sites. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Describe how a **Position Weight Matrix** is used to scan a genome for transcription factor binding sites, with the relevant log-odds scoring equation. **(7 marks)**  
> *— OR —*

> **[Q4. KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Q-style]**  
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **GT–AG rule** for splice site detection. How is a **Hidden Markov Model** used to predict splice sites computationally? **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** A PWM has the following log-odds scores (in bits) for a 4-bp motif:

| Position | A | C | G | T |
|----------|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | +1.20 | -2.10 | -0.30 | -0.80 |
| 2 | -3.00 | +2.15 | -2.50 | -3.10 |
| 3 | -2.40 | -2.00 | +2.20 | -1.90 |
| 4 | -1.80 | -2.30 | -2.10 | +2.05 |

> Score the candidate windows **(i) ACGT**, **(ii) TACG**, and **(iii) CGTA**. Identify which is the most likely true binding site. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solutions

**Q3(a) — TATA box & Kozak sequence (7 marks):**

- **TATA box:** A **TATAAA** consensus located approximately **25–35 bp upstream** of the **transcription start site (TSS)** in eukaryotes. It is recognized by the **TATA-binding protein (TBP)**, a subunit of transcription factor **TFIID**, which recruits RNA polymerase II. Mutations in the TATA box reduce or abolish transcription. **(3.5 marks)**
- **Kozak sequence:** A consensus **gccRccAUGG** (R = purine) flanking the **start codon AUG** in vertebrate mRNAs. The **A at position -3** and the **G at position +4** (relative to the A of AUG) are the most critical. It positions the ribosome and influences translation efficiency; "weak" Kozak contexts can cause reduced protein expression. **(3.5 marks)**

**Q3(b) — Genome scanning with PWM (7 marks):**
1. Slide a window of width $W$ across the genome, one base at a time. **(1 mark)**
2. For each window, look up $S_{i,b}$ for the base $b$ at each position $i$. **(1 mark)**
3. Compute the total score

$$S_{window} = \sum_{i=1}^{W} S_{i, s_i}$$

where $S_{i,b} = \log_2(f_{i,b}^{'} / p_b)$. **(2 marks)**
4. Compare $S_{window}$ to a threshold $T$ (e.g., 80% of the maximum possible score, or a $p$-value cutoff from a dynamic-programming distribution). **(1 mark)**
5. Windows with $S_{window} \geq T$ are predicted as binding sites; their coordinates are reported. **(1 mark)**
6. Practical tools include **MEME** (motif discovery) and **MAST** (motif search). **(1 mark)**

**Q4(a) — GT–AG rule & HMM (7 marks):**
- **GT–AG rule:** Nearly all eukaryotic nuclear introns begin with the dinucleotide **GT** (donor site, 5′ end) and end with **AG** (acceptor site, 3′ end). The donor site is part of a larger consensus **GUAAGU**, and the acceptor is preceded by a polypyrimidine tract and a branch-point **A**. **(3.5 marks)**
- **HMM for splice site detection:** An HMM has states for *exon*, *intron*, *donor*, *acceptor*, and *background*. **Emission probabilities** model nucleotide preferences (e.g., G high in donor, C/T high in polypyrimidine tract). **Transition probabilities** encode the legal order of states. The **Viterbi algorithm** finds the most probable parse of the sequence, simultaneously labeling each base as exon/intron and identifying splice sites where the parser transitions. **(3.5 marks)**

**Q4(b) — Score the three candidate windows (7 marks):**

For window (i) **ACGT**:

$$S_{ACGT} = S_{1,A} + S_{2,C} + S_{3,G} + S_{4,T} = (+1.20) + (+2.15) + (+2.20) + (+2.05) = +7.60 \text{ bits}$$

For window (ii) **TACG**:

$$S_{TACG} = S_{1,T} + S_{2,A} + S_{3,C} + S_{4,G} = (-0.80) + (-3.00) + (-2.00) + (-2.10) = -7.90 \text{ bits}$$

For window (iii) **CGTA**:

$$S_{CGTA} = S_{1,C} + S_{2,G} + S_{3,T} + S_{4,A} = (-2.10) + (-2.50) + (-1.90) + (-1.80) = -8.30 \text{ bits}$$

**Conclusion:** Window **(i) ACGT** with score **+7.60 bits** is the most likely true binding site. Windows (ii) and (iii) are clearly non-sites as their scores are strongly negative. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the pseudocount step** — leads to $-\infty$ log scores for unseen bases. Always include a small $\alpha$ (typically $0.5$–$1.0$).
> 2. **Mixing up log base** — KTU expects $\log_2$ (information in *bits*). Using natural log gives *nats* and wrong numerical values.
> 3. **Forgetting the background model** — log-odds is meaningless without dividing by $p_b$.
> 4. **Independence assumption not stated** — PWM assumes positional independence; HMM/NN relax this. Examiners award a mark for explicitly mentioning it.
> 5. **Skipping the Viterbi equation** in HMM questions — you must write the recursion.
> 6. **Arithmetic slips in numerical problems** — show *every* summation step in the model answer; partial credit is easier to award.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Functional sites** = short DNA signals with biological roles: promoters, TFBS, splice sites, start/stop codons, poly-A signals, CpG islands, RBS.
- **TATA box** ≈ TATAAA at position -25 to -35 from TSS; binds **TBP/TFIID**.
- **Kozak sequence** = gcc**R**ccA**U**G**G** (vertebrate); position -3 (A) and +4 (G) are critical.
- **Shine–Dalgarno** = AGGAGG in prokaryotes; pairs with 16S rRNA.
- **Splice site rule** = GT (donor, 5′) ... AG (acceptor, 3′) for almost all nuclear pre-mRNA introns.
- **Consensus** is a naïve method; **PWM/PSSM** is the standard scoring method; **HMM** handles variable length and dependencies.
- **PWM construction:** count → pseudocount → frequency → log-odds.
- **Score equation:** $S_{i,b} = \log_2(f_{i,b}^{'} / p_b)$; **Window score:** $S = \sum_i S_{i, s_i}$.
- **Information content:** $R_i = 2 - H_i$, max **2 bits/position**; **WebLogo** visualizes this.
- **HMM detection:** **Viterbi algorithm** with states for background, exon, intron, donor, acceptor.
- **Tools to remember:** **MEME / MAST** (PWM), **Genscan / NetGene2** (HMM-based gene/splice finders), **WebLogo** (visualization).
- **Pseudocount** $\alpha$ prevents log-of-zero errors; common choice $\alpha = 0.5$ or $1$.
- **PWM assumes positional independence** — a known limitation that motivates HMMs and deep-learning approaches.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gene Predictions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT615 - BIOINFORMATICS TECHNIQUES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gene Predictions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gene Prediction (Gene Finding)** is a foundational area of computational biology and bioinformatics that deals with the algorithmic identification of functional genomic units — specifically the precise *location, structure, and orientation* of genes and their regulatory elements within a DNA sequence. The formal objective of a gene prediction algorithm is to annotate a raw nucleotide sequence (e.g., `A, C, G, T` over `Σ = {A, C, G, T}`) with biologically meaningful labels such as **exons**, **introns**, **promoters**, **5' UTRs**, **3' UTRs**, **transcription start sites**, **translation initiation sites**, **splice donor/acceptor sites**, and **polyadenylation signals**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Gene prediction is a *core analytical method* in Module 4. Students must clearly distinguish between the **two computational paradigms**: *ab initio (intrinsic)* methods and *homology-based (extrinsic)* methods, and the *hybrid* systems that combine them.

A gene in molecular biology is defined as a **transcribed genomic region** that is translated into a functional product (protein, tRNA, rRNA, etc.). In prokaryotes, gene structure is **continuous** (no introns), while in eukaryotes, gene structure is **discontinuous (split genes)**, containing exons (coding) interrupted by introns (non-coding).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Book Without Spaces" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you are handed a 3-billion-character English novel, but with the following tortures inflicted upon it:
> 1. All spaces and punctuation marks have been removed.
> 2. Every 1000th character is randomly corrupted.
> 3. Large irrelevant advertisements (introns) are inserted between the meaningful sentences (exons).
>
> Your job? **Find where the meaningful sentences (genes) begin, where they end, and what they say.**
>
> This is *exactly* what a gene prediction algorithm does — it must find genes in a **3.2 billion bp human genome** that is mostly non-coding, contains introns, alternative splicing, repetitive elements, and overlapping reading frames. The "spaces" in our analogy are the **stop codons (TAA, TAG, TGA)**, and the "punctuation" are the **splice sites (GT…AG rule)**.

### 1.3 Standard Genomic Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Quantitative Anchors for KTU Examinations:**

| Constant / Metric | Value | Biological Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Human Genome Size** | **~3.2 × 10⁹ bp** | Total nucleotides in haploid human genome |
| **Protein-coding genes (human)** | **~20,000–25,000** | Far fewer than originally predicted (≈100,000) |
| **Exon average length** | **~150 bp** | Size of a typical internal coding exon |
| **Intron average length** | **~3,500 bp** | Why introns dominate genomic "real estate" |
| **Minimum ORF length (typical threshold)** | **100 codons (300 bp)** | Filter for functional ORFs (bacterial: ≥ 100 aa) |
| **Stop codons** | **TAA, TAG, TGA** | Three of 64 codons signal translation termination |
| **Splice donor consensus** | **GT (donor 5' site)** | Begins almost every intron |
| **Splice acceptor consensus** | **AG (acceptor 3' site)** | Ends almost every intron |
| **Kozak consensus (translation init)** | **(gcc)gccRccATGG** | Strong start signal in eukaryotes |
| **Shine-Dalgarno sequence** | **AGGAGG** | Bacterial ribosome binding site |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Gene Prediction

Gene prediction algorithms fall into **two broad computational families**, plus a **hybrid** tier that integrates both.

#### Pillar 1: Ab Initio (Intrinsic / De Novo) Methods

Ab initio methods rely *purely* on the statistical and compositional properties of the query DNA sequence itself. They do **not** require comparison to any external database. The algorithm essentially asks: *"Does this region statistically 'look like' a coding sequence?"*

**Core Sub-Modules of Ab Initio Predictors:**

- **Signal Sensors:** Detect *discrete, short, well-conserved* sequence motifs that flank functional units. Examples: **TATA box** (TATAAA, ~25 bp upstream of TSS), **GC box** (GGGCGG), **Kozak sequence**, **Shine-Dalgarno**, **splice donor/acceptor sites**, **start codons (ATG)**, **stop codons (TAA/TAG/TGA)**.
- **Content Sensors:** Detect *statistical biases* over longer windows. Coding regions (exons) show distinct **codon usage bias**, **hexamer frequency bias**, and **GC content** versus non-coding regions.
- **Hidden Markov Models (HMMs):** The mathematical engine that integrates signals and content into a unified probabilistic framework (e.g., **GENSCAN**, **GeneMark**, **AUGUSTUS**).

#### Pillar 2: Homology-Based (Extrinsic) Methods

These methods predict genes by **comparing the query sequence to experimentally validated genes, ESTs (Expressed Sequence Tags), proteins, or cDNA libraries** in public databases. They exploit evolutionary conservation.

**Sub-Approaches:**

- **Nucleotide-level similarity (BLASTN, BLAT):** Compare genome to ESTs or cDNA — reveals exon structures directly.
- **Protein-level similarity (BLASTX, TBLASTX):** Compare *translated* genome to known proteins — robust against sequencing errors and frameshifts.
- **Comparative genomics (phylogenetic footprinting):** Compare orthologous genomic regions from multiple species to find conserved exons.

#### Pillar 3: Hybrid / Combined Methods

Modern industrial-strength systems fuse ab initio HMMs with homology alignments. Examples: **Twinscan**, **N-SCAN**, **SLAM**, **Augustus+CGP**, **MAKER** pipeline.

### 2.2 Algorithmic Architecture — Hidden Markov Model (HMM) Backbone

A **Hidden Markov Model** is the dominant mathematical framework for ab initio gene prediction. It is a probabilistic state machine defined by the 5-tuple:

$$M = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$$

Where:
- $S$ = finite set of **hidden states** (e.g., 5'UTR, StartCodon, CodingExon, Intron, StopCodon, 3'UTR, Intergenic)
- $V$ = vocabulary of **observable symbols** (the four nucleotides $\{A, C, G, T\}$)
- $A$ = **transition probability matrix** $A = [a_{ij}]$ where $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = s_j \mid q_t = s_i)$
- $B$ = **emission probability matrix** $B = [b_{ik}]$ where $b_{ik} = P(v_k \text{ at time } t \mid q_t = s_i)$
- $\pi$ = **initial state distribution** $\pi_i = P(q_1 = s_i)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HMMs are ideal for gene prediction:** Genes have a *modular, left-to-right grammar* (5'UTR → Start → Exon → Intron → Exon → Stop → 3'UTR). HMMs naturally model this stochastic grammar with hidden states representing the biological modules and observations being the emitted nucleotides.

### 2.3 Critical Sub-Concepts (KTU Hot Topics)

#### 2.3.1 Open Reading Frame (ORF) Detection

An **ORF** is the continuous stretch of codons in a reading frame that begins with a start codon (ATG) and ends with a stop codon in the same reading frame (no in-frame stops interrupting the coding region). The simplest gene prediction is ORF finding:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ORF Length Threshold (T):** A random DNA sequence of length $L$ contains a stop codon on average every $\dfrac{64}{3} \approx 21.3$ codons (i.e., one in-frame stop every ~64 bp). Therefore, a *statistically significant* ORF must be substantially longer than 64 bp. The conventional threshold is **T ≥ 100 codons (300 bp)** for eukaryotic protein-coding genes.

#### 2.3.2 CpG Islands as Promoter Predictors

A **CpG island** is a genomic region of **≥ 200 bp** with a **GC fraction ≥ 0.50** and an **observed/expected CpG ratio ≥ 0.6**. Approximately **60–70% of human gene promoters** are embedded in CpG islands, making them excellent *promoter-region markers* in ab initio pipelines.

#### 2.3.3 Gene Prediction in Prokaryotes vs. Eukaryotes

| Feature | Prokaryotes | Eukaryotes |
|---|---|---|
| Gene density | High (≈ 1 gene / 1000 bp) | Low (≈ 1 gene / 100,000 bp) |
| Introns | Almost none | Abundant (split genes) |
| Algorithm difficulty | Moderate | Very hard |
| Typical tools | **GLIMMER**, GeneMark.hmm, EasyGene | **GENSCAN**, AUGUSTUS, HMMgene, GeneID |
| Overlapping genes | Common (e.g., viruses) | Rare |
| Alternative splicing | Rare | Widespread |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(\text{Exon} \mid \text{sequence } X) = \dfrac{P(X \mid \text{Exon}) \cdot P(\text{Exon})}{P(X)}$ | **Bayes' Theorem** — Posterior probability of a region being an exon |
| 2 | $P(X \mid M) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} b_{q_i}(x_i)$ | **Likelihood** of sequence $X$ under HMM model $M$ |
| 3 | $\log P(X \mid M) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log b_{q_i}(x_i)$ | **Log-likelihood** (numerical stability) |
| 4 | $\alpha_t(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(O_t)$ | **Forward algorithm** recursive step |
| 5 | $\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \cdot b_j(O_{t+1}) \cdot \beta_{t+1}(j)$ | **Backward algorithm** recursive step |
| 6 | $P(q_t = s_i \mid O, M) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid M)}$ | **Posterior state probability** (used in gene prediction to score each nucleotide) |
| 7 | $q^* = \arg\max_q P(q \mid O, M) = \arg\max_q P(q, O \mid M)$ | **Viterbi algorithm** — best parse path |
| 8 | $P(\text{stop codon}) = \dfrac{3}{64}$ | Probability of random in-frame stop |
| 9 | $\text{Expected ORF length (random)} \approx 21 \text{ codons}$ | $\dfrac{64 - 3}{3} \approx 20.3$ |
| 10 | $L_{\text{GC}} = \dfrac{\#G + \#C}{\#A + \#C + \#G + \#T}$ | **GC content** — content sensor for CpG islands |
| 11 | $\text{CpG O/E} = \dfrac{\#CpG}{\#C \times \#G / L}$ | CpG observed/expected ratio |
| 12 | $\text{Sensitivity (Sn)} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of true bases correctly predicted |
| 13 | $\text{Specificity (Sp)} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Fraction of predicted bases that are true |

> **Where:**
> - $TP$ = True Positives (bases correctly labelled as coding)
> - $FN$ = False Negatives (coding bases missed)
> - $FP$ = False Positives (non-coding bases wrongly labelled as coding)
> - $q$ = state path; $O$ = observation sequence; $M$ = HMM model

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Scientific Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why engineers and biotechnologists must master gene prediction:**
> 1. **Precision medicine** — identifying disease-causing mutations in patient genomes.
> 2. **Synthetic biology** — designing minimal genomes and novel gene circuits.
> 3. **Drug discovery** — finding novel drug-target proteins in pathogen genomes.
> 4. **Vaccine design (mRNA)** — selecting stable, highly expressed ORFs.
> 5. **Agricultural biotechnology** — engineering drought/pest-resistant crops.
> 6. **Metagenomics** — annotating genes in environmental microbial samples where lab culture is impossible.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bayesian Posterior for Exon Classification

The *fundamental decision rule* in ab initio gene prediction is: **classify a genomic window $W$ as "exon" if and only if:**

$$P(\text{Exon} \mid W) > P(\text{Not-Exon} \mid W)$$

By **Bayes' Theorem** (Thomas Bayes, 1763):

$$P(\text{Exon} \mid W) = \dfrac{P(W \mid \text{Exon}) \cdot P(\text{Exon})}{P(W)}$$

Similarly:

$$P(\text{Not-Exon} \mid W) = \dfrac{P(W \mid \text{Not-Exon}) \cdot P(\text{Not-Exon})}{P(W)}$$

Since the denominator $P(W)$ is identical in both expressions, the decision rule simplifies to comparing the **numerators**:

$$P(W \mid \text{Exon}) \cdot P(\text{Exon}) \;\overset{?}{>}\; P(W \mid \text{Not-Exon}) \cdot P(\text{Not-Exon})$$

Taking the **logarithm** (monotonic, hence order-preserving) gives the practical log-odds score:

$$S(W) = \log \dfrac{P(W \mid \text{Exon})}{P(W \mid \text{Not-Exon})} + \log \dfrac{P(\text{Exon})}{P(\text{Not-Exon})}$$

$$\boxed{\;S(W) > 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Window } W \text{ is classified as Exon}\;}$$

**Explanatory Note:** The first term captures how well the window matches the *statistical signature* of coding DNA (hexamer frequencies, codon bias). The second term encodes the *a priori* expected density of exons in the genome.

### 3.2 Derivation: Viterbi Algorithm for Best Gene Parse

The **Viterbi algorithm** finds the single most probable state path $q^* = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T)$ through an HMM, which corresponds to the best *gene structure* annotation.

**Initialization (t = 1):**

$$V_1(i) = \pi_i \cdot b_i(O_1)$$

**Recursion (t = 2, 3, …, T):**

$$V_t(j) = \max_{i=1}^{N} \big[\, V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \,\big] \cdot b_j(O_t)$$

**Back-pointer storage (for traceback):**

$$P_t(j) = \arg\max_{i=1}^{N} \big[\, V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \,\big]$$

**Termination:**

$$P^* = \max_{i=1}^{N} V_T(i), \quad q_T^* = \arg\max_{i=1}^{N} V_T(i)$$

**Traceback (t = T − 1, …, 1):**

$$q_t^* = P_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*)$$

**Explanatory Note:** Each $V_t(j)$ is the probability of the *best path* ending in state $j$ at time $t$ that has produced observations $O_1, \ldots, O_t$. The back-pointer $P_t(j)$ records *which previous state* led to this optimum, allowing reconstruction of the full optimal state sequence — the predicted gene structure.

### 3.3 Analytical Step-by-Step: ORF Length Threshold Derivation

A random nucleotide sequence of length $L$ contains 3 possible reading frames per strand. In any single frame, stop codons occur with probability $\dfrac{3}{64}$ per codon position. The expected number of in-frame stops in a window of $n$ codons is:

$$E[\text{stops}] = n \cdot \dfrac{3}{64}$$

The probability that *no* stop codon appears in $n$ consecutive codons (a clean ORF of length $n$) under the null (random) model is:

$$P(\text{clean ORF of length } n) = \left(1 - \dfrac{3}{64}\right)^n = \left(\dfrac{61}{64}\right)^n$$

For a true biological ORF, this probability must be *very small* under the null (so the ORF is statistically surprising). Setting a significance threshold of $\alpha = 0.01$:

$$\left(\dfrac{61}{64}\right)^n \leq 0.01$$

Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$n \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{61}{64}\right) \leq \ln(0.01)$$

$$n \geq \dfrac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(61/64)} = \dfrac{-4.6052}{-0.0479} \approx 96.1$$

Therefore, **$n \geq 100$ codons (300 bp)** is the standard threshold. This explains why gene predictors filter candidate ORFs at $\approx 100$ codons.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — CpG Island Detection

> **Given:** A 250 bp genomic window with the following composition: A = 55, C = 70, G = 65, T = 60.

**Step 1 — Compute GC Content:**

$$L_{\text{GC}} = \dfrac{70 + 65}{55 + 70 + 65 + 60} = \dfrac{135}{250} = 0.54$$

Since $L_{\text{GC}} = 0.54 \geq 0.50$ ✓

**Step 2 — Compute CpG Observed/Expected Ratio:**

Suppose direct counting yields $\#CpG = 14$ observed dinucleotides.

$$\text{CpG O/E} = \dfrac{\#CpG}{\#C \times \#G / L} = \dfrac{14}{70 \times 65 / 250} = \dfrac{14}{18.2} = 0.769$$

Since $\text{CpG O/E} = 0.769 \geq 0.6$ ✓

**Step 3 — Length Check:**

$$L = 250 \text{ bp} \geq 200 \text{ bp} \;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The window **qualifies as a CpG island**, and is likely a promoter region of a gene.

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Algorithm vs. Use Case

| Predictor | Type | Best For | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GENSCAN** | Ab initio (HMM) | Vertebrate genomes | Models GT-AG, GC-isochores |
| **GeneMark** | Ab initio (HMM) | Prokaryotes & eukaryotes | Self-training on input DNA |
| **Glimmer** | Ab initio (IMM) | Microbial genomes | Interpolated Markov Models |
| **AUGUSTUS** | Ab initio (HMM) | Eukaryotes | Trainable on user's own data |
| **Twinscan** | Hybrid | Eukaryotes | Uses *comparative genomics* with a related species |
| **BLASTN vs. ESTs** | Homology | Exon mapping | Direct transcript alignment |
| **GENEWISE** | Homology | Protein-based prediction | Aligns protein to genomic DNA (handles introns) |
| **MAKER** | Hybrid pipeline | Genome annotation projects | Integrates multiple evidence sources |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Gene Prediction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Raw DNA Sequence A C G T] --> B[Preprocessing Module]
    B --> B1[Quality Trimming and Repeat Masking]
    B1 --> C{Choose Prediction Paradigm}
    C --> D[Ab Initio Engine]
    C --> E[Homology Search Engine]
    C --> F[Hybrid Integrator]
    D --> D1[Signal Sensors Promoters Splice Sites Start Stop]
    D --> D2[Content Sensors Codon Bias GC Content Hexamers]
    D --> D3[Hidden Markov Model HMM Parser]
    D1 --> G[Gene Structure Predictions]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    E --> E1[BLASTN vs EST cDNA]
    E --> E2[BLASTX vs Protein DB]
    E --> E3[Comparative Genomics Multi Species]
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F --> F1[Combine HMM with Alignment Evidence]
    F1 --> G
    G --> H[Output Annotated Gene Model]
    H --> H1[Exon Intron Structure]
    H --> H2[UTR Coordinates]
    H --> H3[Translation Product FASTA]
    H --> H4[GFF3 BED Annotation File]
    H1 --> I[Post Processing Validation]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> I1[Compute Sensitivity and Specificity]
    I --> I2[Compare to Benchmarked Set]
    I1 --> J[Final Curated Annotation]
    I2 --> J
```

### 4.2 State Diagram: Hidden Markov Model of a Eukaryotic Gene

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Intergenic
    Intergenic --> Promoter5UTR : TATA Box GC Box
    Promoter5UTR --> UTR5 : Exon Boundary
    UTR5 --> StartCodon : ATG
    StartCodon --> ExonInitial : First Coding Exon
    ExonInitial --> Intron1 : GT Donor Site
    Intron1 --> ExonInternal : AG Acceptor Site
    ExonInternal --> Intron2 : GT Donor Site
    Intron2 --> ExonTerminal : AG Acceptor Site
    ExonTerminal --> StopCodon : TAA TAG TGA
    StopCodon --> UTR3 : Termination
    UTR3 --> PolyA : AAUAAA Signal
    PolyA --> Intergenic : Reset Cycle
    Intron1 --> Intron2 : Direct Intron Extension
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Information Flow in a Gene Predictor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        A1[Raw Genome FASTA]
    end
    subgraph ALGORITHMIC_CORE
        B1[Statistical Model Training]
        B2[ORF Detection]
        B3[Splice Site Scoring]
        B4[Promoter and CpG Analysis]
        B5[Viterbi Decoding]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        C1[Gene Coordinates GFF]
        C2[Translated Protein Sequences]
        C3[Confidence Scores per Base]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A1 --> B4
    B1 --> B5
    B2 --> B5
    B3 --> B5
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    B5 --> C2
    B5 --> C3
```

### 4.4 Comparison Architecture: Ab Initio vs. Homology vs. Hybrid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Query DNA Sequence] --> Q{Sequence Has Close Relative or EST Data?}
    Q -- No --> R1[Ab Initio Only]
    R1 --> R2[GENSCAN GeneMark AUGUSTUS]
    Q -- Yes --> S1[Run Both Pipelines in Parallel]
    S1 --> T1[Ab Initio HMM]
    S1 --> T2[Homology BLASTN BLASTX]
    T1 --> U1[Integrator: Resolve Conflicts Vote Merge]
    T2 --> U1
    U1 --> V1[Hybrid Annotation: MAKER Twinscan]
    R2 --> W[Final Gene Set]
    V1 --> W
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define gene prediction. Distinguish clearly between ab initio and homology-based gene prediction methods with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Gene prediction is the computational process of identifying the precise locations and structures of genes (coding regions, exons, introns, regulatory signals) within a DNA sequence using algorithmic and statistical methods.

**Ab Initio (1 mark):** Ab initio (intrinsic / de novo) methods predict genes *solely* from the statistical and compositional features of the query DNA itself — such as codon usage, GC content, hexamer frequencies, and signal motifs (splice sites, start/stop codons). They do **not** use external databases. *Example: GENSCAN, GeneMark, AUGUSTUS.*

**Homology-based (1 mark):** Homology (extrinsic) methods predict genes by aligning the query sequence against *known* genes, expressed sequence tags (ESTs), cDNA, or proteins from public databases, exploiting evolutionary conservation. *Example: GENEWISE (protein-to-DNA alignment), BLAST-based pipelines.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is an Open Reading Frame (ORF)? Why is a minimum length threshold (typically 100 codons) used in ORF-based gene prediction?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**ORF Definition (1.5 marks):** An Open Reading Frame (ORF) is a continuous stretch of codons in a DNA sequence that begins with a start codon (ATG) and extends in the same reading frame to a downstream in-frame stop codon (TAA, TAG, or TGA), with no intervening stop codons in that frame. It is a strong *candidate* for a protein-coding region.

**Length Threshold Justification (1.5 marks):** A random DNA sequence of length $L$ contains a stop codon, on average, every $\dfrac{64}{3} \approx 21.3$ codons (since 3 of 64 codons are stops). Therefore, ORFs longer than ~21 codons can occur *by chance* in non-coding sequence. Setting a minimum threshold of **100 codons (300 bp)** ensures that the detected ORF is statistically unlikely under a random null model ($P \leq 0.01$), dramatically reducing false positives. This filter is essential because ~95% of a eukaryotic genome is non-coding.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not** write definitions of "gene" and "gene prediction" interchangeably — examiners specifically reward distinction.
> 2. **Do not** omit the *mathematical basis* (Bayes' rule, HMM, Viterbi). A purely descriptive answer in Part B caps your score near 7–8/14.
> 3. **Always** mention the *sensitivity/specificity* trade-off when discussing accuracy.
> 4. **Common error:** Students confuse **CpG islands** with **promoters**. Remember: CpG islands *frequently overlap* promoters, but they are not identical. Be precise.
> 5. **Always** write the formula and then explain it. Showing $\dfrac{3}{64}$ or Bayes' formula earns full method marks even if the calculation is partially incorrect.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Gene Prediction Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 ESE Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the architecture of **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)-based ab initio gene prediction**. Explain the roles of the five HMM parameters, signal sensors, and content sensors. (7 marks)

**(b)** A genomics research team sequences a 500 bp eukaryotic DNA fragment. Composition analysis yields: A = 130, C = 125, G = 115, T = 130. Direct counting reveals 18 CpG dinucleotides. The fragment is flanked by a TATAAA box 28 bp upstream and ends with an in-frame TAA codon. Determine whether this fragment is a strong candidate for the **5' end of a gene**. Justify with quantitative metrics. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — HMM Ab Initio Architecture (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition & Justification (2 marks):**
A Hidden Markov Model (HMM) is a doubly stochastic probabilistic model ideal for gene prediction because genes exhibit a *modular, left-to-right grammar* (5' UTR → start → exon → intron → … → stop → 3' UTR). HMMs can capture this structure with hidden states, even when the precise state boundaries are uncertain.

**Step 2 — The Five Parameters (2 marks):**
1. **States $S$:** Biological modules — $\{5'\text{UTR}, \text{Start}, \text{Exon}_{\text{init}}, \text{Exon}_{\text{int}}, \text{Exon}_{\text{term}}, \text{Intron}, \text{Stop}, 3'\text{UTR}, \text{Intergenic}\}$
2. **Vocabulary $V$:** $\{A, C, G, T\}$
3. **Transitions $A$:** $a_{ij} = P(s_j \mid s_i)$ — probability of moving from one module to the next.
4. **Emissions $B$:** $b_{ik} = P(x_k \mid s_i)$ — probability of emitting nucleotide $x_k$ from state $s_i$ (captures codon bias).
5. **Initial $\pi$:** $\pi_i = P(q_1 = s_i)$ — start state distribution.

**Step 3 — Signal & Content Sensors (2 marks):**
- **Signal sensors** (e.g., **GeneID, GENSCAN**) detect short conserved motifs: splice donor GT, splice acceptor AG, start ATG, stop TAA/TAG/TGA, TATA box, Kozak sequence. They use position-weight matrices (PWMs) to score.
- **Content sensors** capture statistical regularities of coding DNA: codon usage bias, **hexamer frequencies** (61³ ≈ 226,000 possible hexamers; coding sequences show strong biases), GC content.

**Step 4 — Decoding (1 mark):** The Viterbi algorithm is applied to find the optimal state path, yielding the predicted gene structure.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Quantitative 5'-End Gene Assessment (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — GC Content (1 mark):**
$$L_{\text{GC}} = \dfrac{125 + 115}{130 + 125 + 115 + 130} = \dfrac{240}{500} = 0.48$$
Since $0.48 < 0.50$, the *strict* GC criterion is **not met** at the global window level.

**Step 2 — CpG O/E Ratio (1.5 marks):**
$$\text{CpG O/E} = \dfrac{18}{(125 \times 115) / 500} = \dfrac{18}{28.75} = 0.626$$
Since $0.626 \geq 0.6$ ✓, the CpG criterion is **met**.

**Step 3 — Length Check (0.5 marks):**
$L = 500 \text{ bp} \geq 200 \text{ bp}$ ✓

**Step 4 — Upstream TATA Box (1 mark):** The presence of TATAAA at −28 bp is **strong evidence** of a canonical eukaryotic promoter (TATA box typically lies 25–35 bp upstream of the TSS).

**Step 5 — In-frame Stop (1 mark):** The terminal in-frame **TAA** stop codon confirms this fragment is the *3' boundary* of a coding region, supporting that the fragment contains the **5' end (promoter + start) and extends into the gene body**.

**Step 6 — Integrated Conclusion (1 mark):** Despite the marginal global GC content, the combination of *TATA box, high CpG O/E, sufficient length, and terminal stop codon* makes this a **strong candidate for the 5' regulatory region + 5' end of a gene**. Recommendation: run **GENSCAN/AUGUSTUS** with relaxed parameters to confirm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Gene Prediction Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 ESE Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **ab initio** and **homology-based** gene prediction methods. Discuss the role of **ESTs (Expressed Sequence Tags)** and **comparative genomics** in improving eukaryotic gene prediction accuracy. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the aid of a neat labelled diagram, describe the **modular structure of a typical eukaryotic protein-coding gene** as represented in a **gene-prediction HMM**. Explain how **alternative splicing** complicates the prediction problem. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Comparative Gene Prediction (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Ab Initio (1.5 marks):** Predicts genes using *intrinsic* signals (promoter motifs, splice sites) and *content* (codon usage, GC). Strength: works on novel genomes with no relatives. Weakness: poor on short exons, alternative splicing, novel organisms. Examples: GENSCAN, AUGUSTUS.

**Step 2 — Homology-based (1.5 marks):** Predicts genes by aligning query to *known* transcripts/proteins. Strength: very accurate when good matches exist. Weakness: cannot find truly novel genes. Tools: BLASTN, BLASTX, BLAT, GENEWISE.

**Step 3 — Role of ESTs (2 marks):** ESTs are short (200–500 bp) single-pass reads from cDNA libraries. Aligning ESTs to genomic DNA:
- Directly maps **spliced exon boundaries** (introns appear as gaps in the alignment).
- Provides *experimental evidence* of transcription.
- Independent of protein conservation → catches *non-conserved* genes.

**Step 4 — Comparative Genomics (2 marks):** When two related species (e.g., human–mouse) are aligned, *exons* are far more conserved than introns. Algorithms (e.g., **Twinscan**, **PhastCons**) identify conserved blocks → predict exons. This phylogenetic footprinting dramatically reduces false positives, especially for short exons that ab initio predictors miss.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Eukaryotic Gene Structure & Alternative Splicing (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Labelled Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Promoter TATA Box] --> U5[5 Prime UTR]
    U5 --> E1[Exon 1 ~ 150 bp]
    E1 --> I1[Intron 1 ~ thousands of bp]
    I1 --> E2[Exon 2]
    E2 --> I2[Intron 2]
    I2 --> E3[Exon 3]
    E3 --> I3[Intron 3]
    I3 --> E4[Exon 4 Terminal]
    E4 --> U3[3 Prime UTR]
    U3 --> PA[Poly A Signal AAUAAA]
```

**Step 2 — HMM Encoding (2 marks):** Each box in the diagram corresponds to one or more *hidden states* in the gene-prediction HMM. The model emits nucleotide symbols from each state according to the emission matrix $B$, and transitions follow probabilities in $A$ (e.g., an exon-to-intron transition is gated by a GT splice-donor signal sensor).

**Step 3 — Alternative Splicing & Its Complication (2 marks):** Alternative splicing allows a single gene to produce *multiple mRNA isoforms* by selectively including/excluding exons. For example, exon 2 may be skipped in isoform A but included in isoform B. This complicates gene prediction because:
- A single HMM path is no longer sufficient; *multiple valid gene structures* may exist.
- A simple ab initio predictor must output *all* valid parses, increasing algorithmic complexity.
- Tools like **AUGUSTUS** use a *suboptimal path enumeration* to handle this.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Gene Predictions**

- **Gene prediction** = algorithmic identification of genes in DNA sequences.
- **Two paradigms:** *Ab initio* (intrinsic, statistical) vs. *Homology-based* (extrinsic, comparative).
- **HMM** is the dominant mathematical framework; **5-tuple** is $(S, V, A, B, \pi)$.
- **Signal sensors** = short motifs (TATA box, splice GT-AG, Kozak, Shine-Dalgarno).
- **Content sensors** = long-window statistics (codon usage, hexamer frequency, GC content).
- **ORF** = start codon → no in-frame stop → stop codon. **Threshold: 100 codons / 300 bp.**
- **Stop codons:** TAA, TAG, TGA (3 of 64). **Probability of random stop per codon = 3/64.**
- **CpG island:** $\geq 200$ bp, GC $\geq 0.50$, CpG O/E $\geq 0.6$. Marks ~60–70% of human promoters.
- **Bayes' Theorem** is the cornerstone: $P(\text{Exon} \mid X) = \dfrac{P(X \mid \text{Exon}) P(\text{Exon})}{P(X)}$.
- **Viterbi algorithm** finds the single best gene parse path.
- **Sensitivity** = TP / (TP + FN); **Specificity** = TP / (TP + FP).
- **Prokaryotes:** no introns, high gene density → GLIMMER, GeneMark.hmm.
- **Eukaryotes:** split genes, alternative splicing → GENSCAN, AUGUSTUS, HMMgene.
- **Hybrid tools:** MAKER, Twinscan, N-SCAN.
- **Why ORFs are short in random DNA:** mean length ≈ $\dfrac{64}{3} \approx 21$ codons.
- **Key GT-AG rule:** Introns almost universally begin with GT and end with AG.
- **Always state the trade-off** in exam answers: *high sensitivity ↔ lower specificity* and vice versa.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microarray Data Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT615 - BIOINFORMATICS TECHNIQUES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microarray Data Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Microarray Data Analysis** is the systematic, multi-stage computational process of converting raw hybridization intensity signals generated from a DNA microarray experiment into biologically meaningful knowledge about gene expression, genetic variation, and functional genomics. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, it encompasses image quantification, background correction, normalization, statistical filtering for differentially expressed genes (DEGs), clustering, classification, and downstream functional annotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** Microarray data analysis is the process of extracting, normalizing, and interpreting gene expression data obtained from hybridization experiments using statistical and computational methods. It is the bridge between raw signal intensities and biological conclusions.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Probe** | A short DNA sequence (25–70 mer) immobilized on the array surface. |
| **Target** | The fluorescently labeled sample cDNA / cRNA hybridized to probes. |
| **Intensity** | Raw fluorescence reading at a probe location, measured in **arbitrary units (AU)**. |
| **Spot** | A physical location on the array containing millions of copies of one probe. |
| **Differentially Expressed Gene (DEG)** | A gene whose expression level shows a statistically significant change between conditions. |
| **MA Plot** | A scatter plot of log-fold-change (M) vs. mean average log-intensity (A) used to visualize normalization. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Library of Lights"**
>
> Imagine a massive library where every book represents a single gene, and each book has a small light bulb whose brightness is proportional to how often the book is being read in a particular cell.
>
> A **microarray** is like arranging millions of these tiny bulbs on one giant board (the chip). You pour a glowing liquid (the fluorescently labeled sample) over the board. Each gene's cDNA sticks (hybridizes) only to its matching probe, lighting up the corresponding bulb.
>
> A **scanner** then takes a picture of the board. Bright bulbs = highly expressed genes; dim bulbs = silent genes.
>
> **Microarray Data Analysis** is the job of a forensic photographer:
> 1. Cleaning the photo (background correction).
> 2. Adjusting the brightness so all photos are comparable (normalization).
> 3. Comparing the "before disease" photo to the "after disease" photo (differential expression).
> 4. Grouping genes that light up together (clustering).
> 5. Writing the story of what the cell is doing (biological interpretation).
>
> Without analysis, you just have a pretty picture — not a discovery.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in microarray data analysis (highlighted in **bold** as per protocol):

- **Spot diameter ≈ 100–200 μm** on a typical glass slide microarray.
- **Probe length: 25-mer (Affymetrix short oligo), 60–70-mer (Agilent long oligo), or full-length cDNA (500–5000 bp).**
- **Dynamic range: 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻¹⁰⁰** (≈ 4 to 5 orders of magnitude practically, **typically 10² to 10⁵ fold**).
- **Scanning resolution: 5–10 μm/pixel** at **16-bit depth (65,536 intensity levels)**.
- **P-value threshold: ≤ 0.05** (with multiple testing correction).
- **Fold-change threshold: ≥ 2 (i.e., |log₂ FC| ≥ 1)** for biologically meaningful DEGs.
- **False Discovery Rate (FDR) threshold: ≤ 0.05** (Benjamini–Hochberg standard).

---

## 1.4 Scope of Microarray Data Analysis in KTU 2024 Scheme

The KTU Module 4 specifically targets the following learning outcomes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO Mapping (OEBBT615):**
> - **CO4:** Apply bioinformatics tools and techniques for the analysis of high-throughput biological data, including microarray and NGS datasets.
> - **Bloom's Levels Covered:** L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), L5 (Evaluate).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Pipeline of Microarray Data Analysis

Microarray analysis is **not a single algorithm** but a sequential pipeline. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to understand each stage in the order given below.

### Stage 1 — Image Acquisition & Quantification
1. The hybridized array is scanned by a **laser scanner** (e.g., Affymetrix GeneChip Scanner 3000 7G, Agilent DNA Microarray Scanner).
2. Two channels are typically captured:
   - **Cy5 (red, ~650 nm emission)** — Test / treated sample.
   - **Cy3 (green, ~550 nm emission)** — Reference / control sample.
3. Image analysis software (e.g., **GenePix, Agilent Feature Extraction, Affymetrix GCOS**) identifies spot centers, grids, and segments.
4. For each spot, raw intensities are extracted: $I_{Cy5}$ and $I_{Cy3}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The output is a **.gpr (GenePix Result)** or **.txt** file containing spot coordinates, foreground, and background intensities for every probe.

---

### Stage 2 — Background Correction
Background noise arises from non-specific hybridization, dust, and optical artifacts. Two approaches are standard:

| Method | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Local Background Subtraction** | $I_{corrected} = I_{foreground} - I_{background}$ | Can yield negative values — usually floored to a small positive value. |
| **Morphological Opening (affy package)** | $I_{corrected} = I_{foreground} - I_{background\_morph}$ | Robust against non-specific binding. |
| **Model-Based (RMA)** | Estimates background from a normal–exponential convolution | Default in **Robust Multi-array Average (RMA)**. |

---

### Stage 3 — Normalization
Normalization removes systematic biases so that expression distributions are comparable across arrays. The **KTU board paper repeatedly tests this stage**.

#### (a) Global / Scaling Normalization
A single scaling factor is applied to make all arrays have the same mean intensity:

$$S_k = \frac{\mu_{target}}{\mu_k}$$

where $\mu_k$ is the trimmed mean of array $k$ and $\mu_{target}$ is the reference value.

#### (b) Linear (Print-tip) Normalization
Within each print-tip group $g$, perform LOWESS regression on an **M vs. A plot**:

$$M = \log_2\left(\frac{I_{Cy5}}{I_{Cy3}}\right), \quad A = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(I_{Cy5} \cdot I_{Cy3})$$

The LOWESS fit $f(A)$ is subtracted: $M_{normalized} = M - f(A)$.

#### (c) Quantile Normalization (Gold Standard for Affymetrix)
1. Sort each column (array) of the $m \times n$ intensity matrix.
2. Compute the row-wise mean across sorted columns.
3. Replace each column entry with the mean of its rank.
4. Unsort to restore original ordering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Quantile normalization enforces **identical empirical intensity distributions** across all arrays, eliminating most dye and array effects.

#### (d) Z-Score Normalization (per gene)
$$z_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij} - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}$$

where $x_{ij}$ is the log-intensity of gene $i$ in array $j$, $\mu_i$ the gene mean, $\sigma_i$ the gene standard deviation. Used heavily in **clustering and heatmap visualization**.

---

### Stage 4 — Statistical Filtering for Differential Expression

#### Fold Change
$$FC = \frac{I_{treated}}{I_{control}}, \quad \log_2 FC = \log_2\left(\frac{I_{treated}}{I_{control}}\right)$$

DEG criterion: $|\log_2 FC| \geq 1$ (i.e., $\geq 2$-fold change).

#### Student's t-test (two-sample, equal variance)
$$t = \frac{\bar{X}_T - \bar{X}_C}{s_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_T} + \frac{1}{n_C}}}$$

where pooled standard deviation:
$$s_p = \sqrt{\frac{(n_T - 1)s_T^2 + (n_C - 1)s_C^2}{n_T + n_C - 2}}$$

#### Multiple Testing Correction

**Bonferroni Correction (conservative):**
$$\alpha_{adjusted} = \frac{\alpha}{m}$$

where $m$ = number of hypotheses (probesets). For $m = 20{,}000$ and $\alpha = 0.05$, $\alpha_{adjusted} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$.

**Benjamini–Hochberg FDR (preferred):**
1. Order p-values: $p_{(1)} \leq p_{(2)} \leq \dots \leq p_{(m)}$.
2. Find the largest $i$ such that $p_{(i)} \leq \dfrac{i}{m} \cdot q$.
3. Reject all hypotheses $H_{(1)}, \dots, H_{(i)}$ at FDR level $q$ (typically **0.05**).

---

### Stage 5 — Clustering & Pattern Discovery

| Algorithm | Type | Distance Metric | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hierarchical (HCL)** | Unsupervised | Euclidean, Pearson, Spearman | Dendrogram |
| **K-Means** | Unsupervised (partitional) | Euclidean | k cluster centroids |
| **Self-Organizing Maps (SOM)** | Unsupervised (neural) | Euclidean | 2D grid of centroids |
| **PCA** | Linear dimensionality reduction | Covariance / correlation | Principal components |
| **SVM** | Supervised classifier | Kernel (linear, RBF) | Class label |

**Pearson Correlation Distance** between gene expression vectors $x$ and $y$:
$$d_P(x, y) = 1 - r_{xy} = 1 - \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

**K-Means Objective (minimized):**
$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_j} \vert\vert x - \mu_j \vert\vert^2$$

---

### Stage 6 — Functional Annotation & Pathway Enrichment

- **Gene Ontology (GO) term enrichment** using hypergeometric test / Fisher's exact test.
- **KEGG pathway** mapping for metabolic / signaling context.
- **GSEA (Gene Set Enrichment Analysis)** — ranked-list approach using Kolmogorov–Smirnov statistic.

> [!NOTE]
> Tools: **DAVID, g:Profiler, Enrichr, GSEA, BiNGO (Cytoscape plugin).**

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER TABLE — All Formulas Required for KTU Board Examination (Module 4)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **M statistic (log-ratio)** | $M = \log_2(I_{Cy5}/I_{Cy3})$ | Log fold change between two channels. |
| 2 | **A statistic (mean log-intensity)** | $A = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(I_{Cy5} \cdot I_{Cy3})$ | Average log expression level. |
| 3 | **Fold Change (linear)** | $FC = I_{treated}/I_{control}$ | Ratio of intensities. |
| 4 | **Log Fold Change** | $\log_2 FC = \log_2(I_{treated}/I_{control})$ | Symmetric scale for up/down regulation. |
| 5 | **Background-corrected Intensity** | $I_{corr} = I_{fg} - I_{bg}$ | Remove local background. |
| 6 | **Scaling Normalization Factor** | $S_k = \mu_{target}/\mu_k$ | Multiplicative scaling per array. |
| 7 | **Z-Score Normalization** | $z_{ij} = (x_{ij} - \mu_i)/\sigma_i$ | Per-gene standardization. |
| 8 | **Student's t-statistic** | $t = (\bar{X}_T - \bar{X}_C) / \left(s_p \sqrt{1/n_T + 1/n_C}\right)$ | Differential expression test. |
| 9 | **Pooled SD** | $s_p = \sqrt{((n_T-1)s_T^2 + (n_C-1)s_C^2)/(n_T+n_C-2)}$ | Equal-variance t-test denominator. |
| 10 | **Bonferroni α** | $\alpha_{adj} = \alpha/m$ | Conservative multiple-testing correction. |
| 11 | **BH-FDR threshold** | $p_{(i)} \leq (i/m) \cdot q$ | Benjamini–Hochberg rejection rule. |
| 12 | **Euclidean Distance** | $d_E(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Standard metric for clustering. |
| 13 | **Pearson Correlation** | $r_{xy} = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Similarity metric. |
| 14 | **Pearson Distance** | $d_P = 1 - r_{xy}$ | Dissimilarity for clustering. |
| 15 | **K-Means SSE Objective** | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_j} \vert\vert x - \mu_j \vert\vert^2$ | Clustering compactness. |
| 16 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (S2N)** | $S2N = (\mu_1 - \mu_2)/(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)$ | Golub et al. (1999) leukemia study. |
| 17 | **RMA log-intensity** | $\log_2(PM_{corrected})$ | Affymetrix standard. |
| 18 | **Differentially Expressed Gene criterion** | $\vert \log_2 FC \vert \geq 1$ AND $p_{adj} \leq 0.05$ | KTU/standard threshold. |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Scientific Utility

- **Clinical diagnostics:** Cancer subtype classification (e.g., Golub's leukemia dataset, 1999).
- **Drug discovery:** Identifying transcriptional signatures of drug response.
- **Agricultural biotechnology:** Stress-tolerance gene discovery in crops.
- **Personalized medicine:** Patient stratification for therapy selection.
- **Toxicogenomics:** Chemical safety assessment via gene-expression biomarkers.
- **Disease biomarker discovery:** Biomarkers for Alzheimer's, cardiovascular disease, diabetes.

> [!NOTE]
> The 2024 KTU syllabus specifically emphasizes that microarray data analysis is a **prerequisite for understanding RNA-Seq analysis** (covered in later modules), since many concepts (normalization, DEG detection, FDR, clustering) carry over.
